note: Per-port documentation (Phase-4 sweep, T2+T3). `blurb`: the port-panel sentence(s) — a string, or an array of {from,to,text} windows tiling the port's active window when name/ownership changed the port's character. `doc`: documentation-depth paragraphs for research/ports.html. `sources`: citations. build-data injects `blurb` into datasets.ports (era-resolved in the panel); ports.html renders doc + sources. The charter applies: no fabricated precision, and coerced labour named factually and never as value.

blurb.text: Masulipatnam, the great cloth port of the Golconda sultanate — printed and painted Coromandel cottons carried west to Persia and the Red Sea and east to the archipelago.
Madras, the English East India Company's Fort St George — from a strip of sand leased in 1639 to the Company's Coromandel capital and the second city of British India.

doc: The Coromandel coast has no natural harbour: ships lay in an open roadstead off a surf-beaten shore, and cargo and passengers came ashore through the breakers in flat-bottomed masula boats and log catamarans. What the coast had instead was cloth — the painted and printed cottons (chintz, longcloth, salempores) that were the currency of the whole Indian Ocean trade, bought for spices in the archipelago and for gold and silver everywhere.

Before the English fort, the dominant Coromandel port was Masulipatnam, the seaward outlet of the Qutb Shahi sultanate of Golconda, drawing Persian, Armenian, and Golconda merchants and, from the early 1600s, Dutch and English factors. In 1639 the East India Company leased a coastal village and built Fort St George; the town that grew around it — Madras, or Chennapatnam — became the Company's Coromandel headquarters, a fortified 'White Town' beside a weavers' 'Black Town', and after 1746 the object of the Anglo-French wars for the Carnatic.
sources: Sinnappah Arasaratnam, Merchants, Companies and Commerce on the Coromandel Coast, 1650–1740 (1986).
Susan Neild, 'Colonial Urbanism: The Development of Madras City in the 18th and 19th Centuries', Modern Asian Studies 13 (1979).

blurb.text: Jayakarta, a pepper harbour of the Sunda coast under the Sultanate of Banten — a modest roadstead where Chinese junks and local prahus traded before the Dutch came.
Batavia, the walled headquarters of the Dutch East India Company in Asia — the hub from which the VOC ran the spice monopoly, the intra-Asian country trade, and the archipelago.

doc: In 1619 Jan Pieterszoon Coen stormed and burned the town of Jayakarta, a vassal port of the Sultanate of Banten on the Sunda coast, and built in its place a walled Dutch city on the Amsterdam model — canals, a castle, and a grid — named Batavia after the ancestral Batavi of the Netherlands. It became the capital of the VOC's Asian empire and the rendezvous of the retourvloot, the annual fleet home.

Batavia sat at the centre of two trades: the long-haul spice-and-textile run to Amsterdam, and the far larger intra-Asian 'country trade' the Company ran between India, China, Japan, and the islands. Chinese junks brought tea, silk, and porcelain and a large Chinese population built the city's commerce; the surrounding sugar estates worked by that community, and by enslaved and bonded labour drawn from across the archipelago, fed the port. Its canals also made it one of the unhealthiest cities of the age.
sources: Leonard Blussé, Strange Company: Chinese Settlers, Mestizo Women and the Dutch in VOC Batavia (1986).
Femme Gaastra, The Dutch East India Company: Expansion and Decline (2003).

dejima.blurb: Nagasaki, Japan's single window on the West under sakoku — a Japanese shogunal port whose harbour trade ran through the Chinese quarter and the fan-shaped Dutch factory island of Dejima, the one place a European ship might call.
doc: Nagasaki was founded in 1571 by the Ōmura clan as a harbour for the Portuguese carrack trade, and for a few years in the 1580s was even ceded to the Jesuits. After the Tokugawa closed the country (the sakoku edicts of the 1630s), it became Japan's only authorised point of foreign contact — and the sovereign was always Japanese, the shogunate through the Nagasaki bugyō, never Dutch.

Two foreign trades were permitted, both tightly policed. Chinese junks from the Fujian and Jiangnan coast came in numbers, housed after 1689 in a walled Chinese compound (the Tōjin yashiki). The Dutch — the only Europeans allowed after the Portuguese expulsion of 1639 — were confined from 1641 to Dejima, a small artificial island in the harbour, permitted one or two ships a year. The chart shows a single dot: the sovereign is Japan; the Dutch flag flew only over the leased island, and the ships that called flew their own colours.
sources: Marius B. Jansen, The Making of Modern Japan (2000), ch. on the Tokugawa foreign relations.
Leonard Blussé et al. (eds), The Deshima Dagregisters (Dutch factory registers, NL-HaNA 1.04.21).

blurb.text: A cluster of Portuguese-held islands (Bombaim) on the Konkan coast — minor beside the Estado da Índia's capital at Goa to the south, which the chart's dot stands in for in this era.
Bombay, the English East India Company's western presidency — the deep, sheltered harbour that made it the Company's naval base and, by the 1800s, the great cotton and opium port of western India.

doc: The seven islands of Bombay were a minor Portuguese holding until they passed to the English Crown in 1661 as part of the dowry of Catherine of Braganza, and to the East India Company on a nominal rent in 1668. Its magnificent natural harbour — deep, sheltered, and defensible — made it the Company's western naval base and the seat of the Bombay Presidency.

Bombay rose as Surat, the older Mughal port to the north, declined. It became the western terminus of the Company's country trade — cotton and, increasingly, Malwa opium carried to China — and the home of the Bombay Marine and a shipbuilding industry manned by Parsi master-builders. In this chart the dot is era-named Goa before 1661: Goa proper, the Portuguese Estado da Índia's capital, lies some 400 km south, and the roster stages its counted Carreira-da-Índia terminus here until its own promotion.
sources: Om Prakash, European Commercial Enterprise in Pre-Colonial India (1998).
Lakshmi Subramanian, Indigenous Capital and Imperial Expansion: Bombay, Surat and the West Coast (1996).

blurb.text: Hugli, the Mughal river-port of Bengal — the Ganges delta's outlet for muslins, silk, and saltpetre, worked by Mughal officials and Portuguese, Dutch, and English factors upriver.
Calcutta, the East India Company's Bengal settlement and, after Plassey, the capital of British India — the great export port of the richest province of the subcontinent.

doc: The trade of Bengal — the finest cotton muslins, raw silk, and saltpetre for Europe's gunpowder — ran through river ports on the Hooghly, the westernmost distributary of the Ganges. The Mughal customs port was Hugli; the Portuguese, Dutch, and English all held factories along the river before the Company settled the villages that became Calcutta in 1690.

After the Company's victory at Plassey (1757) gave it the revenues of Bengal, Calcutta became the capital of British India and one of the great ports of the world, exporting the province's textiles and later its opium, indigo, and jute. The chart stages the dot at the Hooghly mouth for the 1° grid; the real city lies some 130 km upriver, reached by pilots through the treacherous James and Mary shoals.
sources: Om Prakash, The Dutch East India Company and the Economy of Bengal, 1630–1720 (1985).
P. J. Marshall, East Indian Fortunes: The British in Bengal in the Eighteenth Century (1976).

mocha.blurb: The Red Sea coffee port whose beans supplied Europe before the plantations of Java and the Antilles, shipped under an Ottoman flag that the Zaydi imams of Yemen contested through much of the era.
doc: Mocha (al-Mukha), on Yemen's Red Sea coast, was an open roadstead rather than a true harbour: ships anchored offshore and lightered their goods over the shoals to the town. It was the great emporium of Yemeni coffee, grown in the highland terraces inland and brought down to the coast for export. Ottoman and Egyptian merchants carried it up the Red Sea to Suez and Cairo for the Middle Eastern market; from the mid-seventeenth century the English, Dutch, and French East India companies opened factories at Mocha to buy coffee directly for Europe, alongside the Gujarati and other Indian Ocean traders who had long frequented the port.

Ottoman from 1538, Mocha's coffee monopoly reached its height between about 1650 and 1720, even though the Zaydi imams of Yemen expelled the Ottoman garrisons in 1636 and governed the country thereafter (a change of ruling power this chart cannot flag for want of a Zaydi polity id). The port declined once coffee cultivation spread to Dutch Java, French Bourbon, and the Caribbean in the eighteenth century and undercut the Yemeni monopoly; by the early nineteenth century the trade had thinned and shifted north toward Hodeida and Aden, though 'Mocha' survived as a name for the coffee itself.
sources: Nancy Um, The Merchant Houses of Mocha: Trade and Architecture in an Indian Ocean Port (2009)
C. G. Brouwer, Cauwa ende Comptanten: The Dutch East India Company in Yemen, 1614-1655 (1988)

muscat.blurb: The fortified harbour of the Ya'rubid and then Al Bu Sa'idi sultans of Oman - the base from which an Arab sea power drove the Portuguese from the Gulf and the Swahili coast and built a commercial empire of dhows.
doc: Muscat is a deep natural harbour ringed by bare mountains on the Gulf of Oman, its entrance guarded by the twin forts Jalali and Mirani (built by the Portuguese, taken by the Omanis in 1650). It commanded the mouth of the Persian Gulf and the trade linking Arabia, Persia, India, and East Africa. Omani seafarers and resident Indian merchants, chiefly Gujarati Banians, handled dates, dried fish, Gulf pearls, and the carrying trade in Indian textiles - and, named soberly, the traffic in enslaved East Africans carried from the Swahili coast into Arabia and the Gulf.

The Ya'rubid imams expelled the Portuguese from Muscat in 1650 and carried the war to them in East Africa, taking Mombasa in 1698. Under the Al Bu Sa'idi dynasty from 1749 Oman became the dominant Arab maritime power of the western Indian Ocean; Sayyid Said (r. 1804-56) ruled a commercial empire that spanned Muscat and Zanzibar, and in 1840 he moved his court to Zanzibar as the clove-and-ivory economy of the African side came to eclipse Muscat itself.
sources: Patricia Risso, Oman and Muscat: An Early Modern History (1986)
Abdul Sheriff, Slaves, Spices and Ivory in Zanzibar (1987)

jedda.blurb: Mecca's sea gate on the Red Sea - the port of the pilgrimage and the terminus of the spice and coffee trade from the Indian Ocean, held for the Ottoman sultan as protector of the Holy Cities.
doc: Jeddah (Jidda), the port of the Hijaz, lay behind a coral reef whose difficult approach local pilots knew intimately. It served Mecca, some seventy kilometres inland, and every year received by sea the pilgrims of the hajj from Egypt, India, and the Malay world; its customs were among the richest in the Ottoman Red Sea. Ottoman, Egyptian, Hadhrami, Indian, and Southeast Asian merchants traded the coffee coming up from Mocha, Indian textiles, and the pepper and spices that Aceh and other Indian Ocean ports shipped direct to the Red Sea, outflanking the Cape route.

Ottoman from 1517, when Selim I took Egypt and with it the guardianship of the Holy Cities, Jeddah was governed under Ottoman suzerainty by the Sharifs of Mecca, who shared its customs revenue. It prospered as long as the Red Sea route drew a share of the Indian trade, and it kept its essential function - the harbour of the pilgrimage - throughout the era, even as the Wahhabi-Saudi expansion and Muhammad Ali of Egypt's intervention convulsed the Hijaz in the early nineteenth century.
sources: Suraiya Faroqhi, Pilgrims and Sultans: The Hajj under the Ottomans, 1517-1683 (1994)
William Facey (ed.), Jeddah: Port of Mecca, Gateway of the Indian Ocean Trade (2005)

blurb.text: A Swahili city-state on the East African coast held by Portugal from Fort Jesus - a link in the Estado da India's chain guarding the sea road to India and the gold of Sofala.

The Swahili port after the Omani conquest of 1698, held for much of the eighteenth century by the semi-independent Mazrui governors - a station of the ivory caravans and of the East African trade in enslaved people carried into Arabia.

doc: Mombasa sits on an island with two deep harbours and was one of the leading Swahili city-states of the East African coast: Muslim, Swahili-speaking, and tied by dhow to Arabia, the Gulf, and India on the monsoon. Its trade was ivory and other goods brought from the interior, mangrove poles, and grain, exchanged for Indian cloth and Gulf dates. The Portuguese raised Fort Jesus in 1593 to command the town and the route to India. Throughout the era, and increasingly under Omani rule, the coast also trafficked enslaved East Africans - men, women, and children taken from the interior and carried against their will into Arabia, the Gulf, and the islands.

Portugal's hold on Mombasa was contested through the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries; the Omani Arabs, having recovered Muscat, besieged and finally took Fort Jesus in 1698, ending the main Portuguese presence north of Mozambique. Under Oman the Mazrui family governed the town semi-independently for much of the eighteenth century, resisting the Busaidi sultans until Zanzibar's Sayyid Said subdued them in 1837. As the nineteenth-century clove and ivory boom centred on Zanzibar, and as the Atlantic slave trade was suppressed, the East African trade in captives grew - a fact named here plainly, never as commerce.
sources: Justus Strandes, The Portuguese Period in East Africa (trans. 1961)
Abdul Sheriff, Slaves, Spices and Ivory in Zanzibar (1987)

blurb.text: A Swahili island off the East African coast within the Portuguese sphere of the Estado da India - a watering and trading call on the monsoon route to Goa.

The African seat of the Omani sultanate - Sayyid Said moved his court here in 1840 - and the hub of the clove plantations, the ivory caravans, and the East African trade in enslaved people that grew as the Atlantic one was suppressed.

doc: Zanzibar is a low coral island with a sheltered anchorage on its western side, and it lay at the heart of the Swahili world, its stone town trading by dhow with Arabia, the Gulf, and India on the monsoon. Under the Omani Busaidi sultans, and especially after Sayyid Said transferred his capital there in 1840, it became the commercial capital of the western Indian Ocean: clove plantations, ivory brought down by caravan from the interior lakes, and gum copal and other African produce, all exchanged for American cloth ('merikani'), Indian textiles, and Gulf goods, with Indian Banian and Bohra financiers underwriting the trade.

That nineteenth-century clove and ivory boom rested on the coerced movement of enslaved East Africans - captured in the interior, marched to the coast, held and sold in Zanzibar's market, and set to labour on the island's plantations or carried on to Arabia, the Gulf, and India. As the Atlantic trade was suppressed after 1807, this East African traffic in human beings expanded through the first half of the century. It is named here as what it was: people trafficked and held against their will, never a commodity and never a source of profit to be tallied.
sources: Abdul Sheriff, Slaves, Spices and Ivory in Zanzibar: Integration of an East African Commercial Empire into the World Economy, 1770-1873 (1987)
M. Reda Bhacker, Trade and Empire in Muscat and Zanzibar (1992)

mozambique.blurb: Ilha de Mocambique - Portugal's fortified island call on the East African coast, the administrative seat of the Estado da India south of the line and the Carreira's watering place on the run to Goa.
doc: Mozambique Island, a small coral island in a good harbour, was the Portuguese Crown's chief base on the East African coast, dominated by the great fortress of Sao Sebastiao (from 1558). Outward and homeward Indiamen on the Carreira da India called here to water, refit, and wait out the monsoon, and it was the residence of the captain-general of Portuguese East Africa. Its own trade was the gold and ivory brought down from the Zambezi hinterland through the prazo estates, exchanged for the Indian cloth that Gujarati merchants carried.

Portuguese from 1507, the island withstood a Dutch siege in 1607-08 and remained the colonial capital until 1898. Its Zambezi gold trade declined over the era, but from the later eighteenth century Mozambique and the neighbouring coast became a major source in the slave trade - enslaved Africans shipped to the Mascarenes, to Brazil, and to the Rio de la Plata - a coerced traffic named here soberly, while ivory remained a staple export to India.
sources: Malyn Newitt, A History of Mozambique (1995)
M. D. D. Newitt, Portuguese Settlement on the Zambesi (1973)

male.blurb: The Maldive sultanate's island capital and the source of the cowrie shells that served as small change across the Bay of Bengal and, carried by way of Europe, on the slave coasts of West Africa.
doc: Male, the seat of the Maldive sultans, was the political and trading centre of the low coral atolls strung across the equatorial Indian Ocean. The islands' great export was the cowrie shell (Cypraea moneta), gathered in the lagoons and shipped in bulk to Bengal, where it circulated as low-denomination currency, together with coir rope twisted from the coconut husk and prized for rigging, and dried 'Maldive fish' sent to Ceylon and India. The trade was carried in Maldivian, Bengali, and Gujarati bottoms on the monsoon.

The sultanate kept its independence through most of the era, apart from a brief Portuguese domination in the mid-sixteenth century (ended in 1573 by Muhammad Thakurufaanu) and later spells of nominal Dutch and then British protection exercised from Ceylon. The cowrie trade took on a grimmer dimension when European traders bought Maldive shells in Bengal and re-exported them to West Africa, where cowries served as currency in the purchase of enslaved people - a connection named here plainly. The traffic in shells persisted into the nineteenth century.
sources: Jan Hogendorn and Marion Johnson, The Shell Money of the Slave Trade (1986)
Clarence Maloney, People of the Maldive Islands (1980)

blurb.text: The French Indian Ocean naval base and entrepot on Ile de France, developed by Mahe de La Bourdonnais and, in the revolutionary wars, the corsair harbour that preyed on British India shipping.
Port Louis of British Mauritius after the capture of 1810 - a sugar colony and a call on the Cape route, worked latterly by indentured labour from India.

doc: Port Louis, on the sheltered northwest coast of the volcanic island the French called Ile de France (Mauritius), was the great harbour of the Mascarenes. From 1735 the French East India Company governor Mahe de La Bourdonnais built it into the naval and commercial base of French power in the Indian Ocean, with dockyards, warehouses, and fortifications; it provisioned Indiamen, served as a naval station, and grew sugar, coffee, and cotton on estates worked by enslaved Africans and Malagasy carried to the island - a coerced traffic named here soberly.

During the revolutionary and Napoleonic wars Port Louis was the base of the French frigates and privateers that raided British East India Company shipping, until a British expedition captured the island in December 1810; it became the colony of Mauritius, Port Louis its capital, confirmed to Britain in 1814. After the abolition of slavery in 1835 the sugar planters imported large numbers of indentured labourers from India, who arrived through Port Louis - the beginning of the indenture migration - and the port kept its importance as a strategic call on the route round the Cape.
sources: Auguste Toussaint, History of Mauritius (trans. 1977)
Richard B. Allen, Slaves, Freedmen and Indentured Laborers in Colonial Mauritius (1999)

basra.blurb: The head of the Persian Gulf and the Ottoman terminus of the Baghdad caravan route - the Gulf's link to Aleppo and the Mediterranean, and a rival to Persian Bandar Abbas for the India trade.
doc: Basra lay far up the Shatt al-Arab, the confluence of the Tigris and Euphrates; sea-going ships anchored at the bar and worked up the river to the town. It was the Ottoman Empire's port at the head of the Gulf and the maritime end of the great caravan route to Baghdad and Aleppo, by which Indian and Gulf goods reached the Mediterranean. The English and Dutch East India companies kept factories there, and the trade - dates, Persian and Indian textiles, horses, and pearls from the Gulf fisheries - passed largely through Armenian, Jewish, Indian, and Arab merchant houses.

Ottoman from 1546, Basra's fortunes rose and fell with the security of the caravan roads and the rivalry with Safavid, then Qajar, Persia across the Gulf; it was briefly held by the Persians (1697-1701) and besieged and taken by Karim Khan Zand in 1775-79. It gained trade whenever war closed the alternatives - the overland Aleppo road or Persian Bandar Abbas - and the East India Company made it an important station on the Gulf and on the packet route carrying mail overland to Europe.
sources: Thabit A. J. Abdullah, Merchants, Mamluks, and Murder: The Political Economy of Trade in Eighteenth-Century Basra (2001)
Rudi Matthee, The Politics of Trade in Safavid Iran: Silk for Silver, 1600-1730 (1999)

bandar-abbas.blurb: Safavid Persia's Gulf outlet from 1622, when Shah Abbas took Hormuz from the Portuguese with English help - the port through which Persian silk went to sea, declining after the Safavid collapse.
doc: Bandar Abbas (Gombroon to the Europeans), on the Persian shore opposite the island of Hormuz, became Safavid Persia's principal Gulf port after Shah Abbas I, with English East India Company naval help, expelled the Portuguese from Hormuz in 1622. Both the English and the Dutch companies kept substantial factories there. Its trade was Persian silk - the shah's monopoly export - together with Kerman wool and carpets and dried fruit, exchanged for Indian textiles, spices, and specie; it was also a hub of the Indian 'country trade' within the Gulf and to the Malabar and Gujarat coasts.

The port flourished under the later Safavids as the outlet for the royal silk trade, but it declined sharply after the Afghan invasion and the fall of the Safavid dynasty in 1722 threw Persia into disorder. The European companies ran down their Gombroon factories, and by the mid-eighteenth century much of the Gulf trade had shifted to Ottoman Basra and to Bushire under the Zands; Bandar Abbas never recovered its former importance.
sources: Rudi Matthee, The Politics of Trade in Safavid Iran: Silk for Silver, 1600-1730 (1999)
Willem Floor, The Persian Gulf: A Political and Economic History of Five Port Cities, 1500-1730 (2006)

blurb.text: The Crimean Khanate's chief Black Sea port under Ottoman protection - a great emporium of the northern trade and one of the largest slave markets of the early-modern world, where captives seized in raids on Slavic and Caucasian lands were held and sold.
Renamed Feodosia on Russia's annexation of the Crimea in 1783, which ended the khanate and the trade in captives it had run.

doc: Kaffa (Caffa; later Feodosia), on the southeastern coast of the Crimean peninsula, had a fine harbour and had been the great Genoese trading colony of the Black Sea before the Ottomans took it in 1475. Under the Crimean Khanate - a Tatar state that was an Ottoman vassal - it was the chief port of the northern Black Sea, shipping grain, salt, hides, and wax up to Istanbul. It was also one of the principal slave markets of the early-modern world: the Crimean Tatars raided the Slavic lands of Poland-Lithuania and Muscovy and the peoples of the Caucasus, and the captives they seized - men, women, and children - were driven to Kaffa, held there, and sold on into the Ottoman lands. This traffic in human beings is named here as what it was, never as trade and never as profit.

The scale of the raiding was immense: over the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries it carried away very large numbers of people from Ukraine, Poland, and Russia, and Kaffa was its main outlet, remembered in a Ukrainian lament as the place where the captives were sold. Russian and Ottoman wars broke the khanate's power in the eighteenth century, and Catherine II annexed the Crimea in 1783, ending the khanate, the raids, and the slave market; the port continued under Russia as Feodosia - the point at which its trade as represented on this chart closes.
sources: Alan W. Fisher, 'Muscovy and the Black Sea Slave Trade', Canadian-American Slavic Studies 6 (1972)
Mikhail Kizilov, 'Slave Trade in the Early Modern Crimea from the Perspective of Christian, Muslim, and Jewish Sources', Journal of Early Modern History 11 (2007)

arkhangelsk.blurb: Russia's ice-bound White Sea door, the tsars' one seagoing outlet for furs, tallow, hemp and naval stores until St Petersburg supplanted it after 1703.
doc: Arkhangelsk stood at the mouth of the Northern Dvina, sixty-odd kilometres up a marshy delta on the shore of the White Sea, and was founded in 1584 as Russia's first true maritime port. Its harbour was open water for only the short summer-and-autumn ice season; for the rest of the year the Dvina and the Gorlo throat of the White Sea were frozen, so the whole of the town's foreign trade compressed into a single annual fair when the English Muscovy Company, the Dutch, and later Hamburg and other carriers came up on the summer wind. The approach itself was a feat of pilotage through shoal and ice, and no vessel dared a winter departure — a seasonality the chart preserves.

For more than a century Arkhangelsk was the sole channel of Muscovy's seaborne commerce with western Europe: outward went furs, hides, tallow, wax, potash, hemp, flax, cordage and ship's timber — the naval stores on which the western fleets depended — and inward came silver, cloth, metals and manufactures. Its fortunes turned on a decision of state rather than of the sea. When Peter I took the Baltic coast and built St Petersburg, he deliberately diverted the trade south to his new capital, restricting Arkhangelsk's commerce by decree from 1722; the port declined into a regional and Arctic role, reviving only partially in the later eighteenth century. It remained active as a working harbour throughout the chart's window, but never again as Russia's chief gate to Europe.
sources: Jarmo T. Kotilaine, Russia's Foreign Trade and Economic Expansion in the Seventeenth Century (Brill, 2005)
T. S. Willan, The Early History of the Russia Company, 1553-1603 (Manchester University Press, 1956)

smeerenburg.blurb: 'Blubber Town', the Dutch shore whaling settlement on Spitsbergen's Amsterdam Island (c.1614-1660), where bowhead blubber was flensed and boiled before the bay whales gave out and the fishery moved offshore.
doc: Smeerenburg — Dutch for 'Blubber Town' — was not a sovereign port but a seasonal shore station on the low spit of Amsterdam Island at the north-west corner of Spitsbergen, near 80 degrees north, established around 1614 and worked chiefly by the Noordsche Compagnie. It was a summer settlement of try-works, coopers' sheds, warehouses and dwellings occupied only during the whaling season; no ship reached it in winter, and the popular later legend of a bustling town of thousands with bakeries and a fort is an exaggeration corrected by twentieth-century archaeology, which found a modest cluster of blubber-boiling ovens and cabins for a few hundred men at its height.

The station existed to exploit the Greenland right whale (bowhead) that gathered in the bays along the Spitsbergen coast, where they could be killed from small boats close inshore, towed to the beach, flensed, and their blubber rendered to oil in the great copper kettles for the European lighting and soap markets. That bay-whaling model was Smeerenburg's making and its undoing: as the near-shore whales were hunted out over the 1630s and 1640s the catch moved into the open pack ice, where oil was increasingly boiled at sea or brought home raw, and the fixed shore station lost its purpose. Smeerenburg was abandoned by about 1660, ending as a site of early Arctic industrial over-exploitation rather than a lasting town.
sources: Louwrens Hacquebord, Smeerenburg: het verblijf van Nederlandse walvisvaarders op de westkust van Spitsbergen in de zeventiende eeuw (Rijksmuseum, dissertation, 1984)
Chr. de Jong, Geschiedenis van de oude Nederlandse walvisvaart, 3 vols (1972-1979)

blurb.text: The bowhead whaling grounds between Greenland and Baffin Island, worked from the 1720s chiefly by the Dutch Greenland fleet as the near-Spitsbergen fishery gave out.
The same Davis Strait bowhead grounds, dominated after the Dutch collapse by the British and Scottish fleet out of Hull, Peterhead and Dundee.

doc: Davis Strait is not a port but a whaling ground — the stretch of ice-strewn water between the west coast of Greenland and Baffin Island, named for the Elizabethan navigator John Davis, where the Greenland right whale (bowhead) could be hunted along the floe edge and in the open bights of the pack. As the whales near Spitsbergen were thinned out in the later seventeenth century, the fleets followed them west, and from the 1720s the strait became the principal Arctic fishery. It is entered here as a grounds node, on the precedent of Smeerenburg: ships did not call at a town but worked a season on the ice and sailed home, which is why the chart treats it as a destination without a harbour.

The fishery changed hands over the chart's window, which is why the grounds carry a shift of dominant flag rather than a sovereignty. Through much of the eighteenth century the Dutch Greenland and Straat Davis fleet was the largest presence; the Dutch effort was broken by the Fourth Anglo-Dutch War (1780-84) and the wars that followed, and had all but collapsed by 1800. The British fishery rose to take its place, first from Hull and the English east-coast ports and then overwhelmingly from the Scottish ports of Peterhead, Aberdeen and Dundee, which worked the strait deep into the nineteenth century and pushed north into Baffin Bay as the accessible whales were killed off. The trade produced oil and whalebone for European industry and left the bowhead stock severely depleted by the end of the period.
sources: Gordon Jackson, The British Whaling Trade (A. & C. Black, 1978)
W. Gillies Ross, Arctic Whalers, Icy Seas: Narratives of the Davis Strait Whale Fishery (Irwin, 1985)

york-factory.blurb: The Hudson's Bay Company's chief entrepot on the Bay, at the mouth of the Hayes River from 1684 — furs out and trade goods in on one or two ships a year through a few ice-free weeks, and the head of the York-boat inland transport system.
doc: York Factory stood on the low, boggy point between the Hayes and Nelson rivers on the south-western shore of Hudson Bay, established by the Hudson's Bay Company in 1684. It was a fur-trade entrepot rather than a settlement town: a palisaded post whose whole seaborne life turned on the annual arrival of the Company's one or two ships, which had to reach the Bay, unload English trade goods, load the season's furs and clear the ice before freeze-up, a window of only a few weeks. From York the goods moved inland by river, and in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries by the purpose-built York boat — a heavy inland freighter, rowed and portaged, that replaced the canoe on the routes to the interior posts and gave the fur brigades their reach across the Northwest.

The furs — prime beaver above all, with marten, fox and other pelts — came down to the Bay through Indigenous middlemen, chiefly Cree and Assiniboine, whose trade the post depended on entirely; York Factory was a European foothold on the edge of Indigenous country, not a colony over it. Its early decades were contested with France: in the wars of the 1680s-1710s the Bay posts changed hands repeatedly, and York itself was taken by Pierre Le Moyne d'Iberville in 1694, retaken, seized again, and held by France for much of 1697-1714 until the Treaty of Utrecht restored the whole Bay to Britain and the Company. Thereafter York Factory was the Company's principal depot and administrative headquarters on the Bay for well over a century, declining only as inland routes and rival transport supplanted the Hayes River road in the mid-nineteenth century.
sources: E. E. Rich, The History of the Hudson's Bay Company, 1670-1870, 2 vols (Hudson's Bay Record Society, 1958-1959)
Michael Payne, The Most Respectable Place in the Territory: Everyday Life in Hudson's Bay Company Service, York Factory, 1788-1870 (Canadian Parks Service, 1989)

sitka.blurb: New Archangel (Novo-Arkhangelsk), the Russian-American Company's colonial capital on Tlingit land from 1799/1804, the depot of the North Pacific sea-otter fur trade for the Canton market.
doc: Sitka, called Novo-Arkhangelsk (New Archangel) by the Russians, sits on Baranof Island in the Alexander Archipelago of south-east Alaska, on the country of the Sitka Tlingit (Kiks.adi), who were and remained the sovereign people of the place. This must be named plainly: the Russian-American Company did not settle empty land. Aleksandr Baranov built the first fort near the site in 1799, and in 1802 the Sitka Tlingit destroyed it and killed or drove off its occupants; the Company returned in force in 1804, took the position after the battle at Indianski/Shis'ki Noow, and made New Archangel the capital of Russian America. Tlingit presence and pressure on the post never ceased. The harbour was a deep-water sound that let ocean-going ships lie close under the fort, and the town grew into the administrative and shipbuilding centre of the whole colony.

The colony existed for one commodity above all: the pelt of the sea otter, the most valuable fur in the world, sold through Kyakhta and increasingly shipped direct to Canton for the China market. That trade rested on coercion. The Russian-American Company compelled Aleut (Unangax) and Alutiiq hunters, whose skill with the kayak the Russians could not match, to hunt otter for the Company under a system of hostage-taking, forced labour and reprisal that killed many and broke whole communities. The hunt exterminated the sea otter across most of its range within a few decades, so that by the mid-nineteenth century the fur economy that built Sitka had collapsed for want of animals — an early and near-total commercial extinction. Sitka remained the colonial capital until Russia sold Alaska to the United States in 1867, past the chart's horizon.
sources: Lydia T. Black, Russians in Alaska, 1732-1867 (University of Alaska Press, 2004)
Ilya Vinkovetsky, Russian America: An Overseas Colony of a Continental Empire, 1804-1867 (Oxford University Press, 2011)

nootka.blurb: Nootka Sound on Vancouver Island, in Mowachaht/Nuu-chah-nulth country under the chief Maquinna — the anchorage where American, British and Spanish maritime fur traders watered and bought sea-otter pelts for Canton, and the flashpoint of the 1789-94 Nootka Crisis.
doc: Nootka Sound is an inlet on the west coast of Vancouver Island, and it was and is Mowachaht territory, part of the Nuu-chah-nulth world; in the late eighteenth century the summer village of Yuquot at its mouth was the seat of the chief Maquinna, whose people controlled who might anchor and trade there. The maritime fur traders who crowded in after James Cook's crews found the value of sea-otter pelts in 1778 — Americans out of Boston, British private ships, and others — came at Maquinna's sufferance, watering, refitting and bartering iron, copper and cloth for otter skins to sell in Canton. The harbour was theirs to use only on Mowachaht terms, and the chart flags the anchorage to the Mowachaht as a shore power for exactly that reason: the trading ships fly American and British colours, but the ground beneath them does not become theirs.

The sound gave its name to the Nootka Crisis of 1789-94, when Spain, asserting an old claim to the whole Pacific coast, seized British trading vessels there and briefly built a garrison at Yuquot; the affair brought Britain and Spain to the edge of war before the Nootka Conventions settled it in Britain's favour and Spain withdrew, an episode that mattered chiefly to the two European empires and left Mowachaht sovereignty over the place formally untouched. The fur trade itself faded not by any treaty but with its quarry: as the sea otter was hunted toward extinction along the coast the ships stopped coming, and Nootka's role as the great rendezvous of the trade ended in the 1810s-1840s, the anchorage falling quiet as the animal that drew the world to it disappeared.
sources: Robin Fisher, Contact and Conflict: Indian-European Relations in British Columbia, 1774-1890 (University of British Columbia Press, 1977)
Warren L. Cook, Flood Tide of Empire: Spain and the Pacific Northwest, 1543-1819 (Yale University Press, 1973)

blurb.text: Älvsborg, the fortified Swedish harbour at the mouth of the Göta, was the kingdom's one narrow outlet to the western sea between the Danish-held provinces.
Chartered in 1621, Gothenburg became Sweden's Atlantic gateway and home port of the Swedish East India Company's Canton tea voyages.

doc: Gothenburg stands at the mouth of the Göta älv on the Kattegat, the single point where the Swedish crown could reach the western sea when Halland, Bohuslän and Skåne were still Danish or Norwegian ground; the older harbour clustered under the guns of Älvsborg fortress, which guarded the estuary and was twice ransomed from Denmark. The city proper was chartered by Gustavus Adolphus in 1621 and laid out on a Dutch plan of straight canals by Dutch engineers, and it is from that founding that the chart's dot changes name from Älvsborg to Gothenburg.

Its early exports were the staples of the Swedish economy — bar iron, timber and tar carried to the Dutch and the British — later joined by a great herring boom in the last third of the eighteenth century. Its distinctive fortune, however, was the Swedish East India Company, chartered in 1731 and sailing from Gothenburg to Canton for tea, porcelain and silk, much of the tea re-exported (and smuggled) into Britain; the neutral Swedish flag let the port prosper as a carrier through the wars that idled its rivals.
sources: Christian Koninckx, The First and Second Charters of the Swedish East India Company (1731–1766) (1980)
David Kirby, The Baltic World 1492–1772 (1990)

danzig.blurb: Danzig, the self-governing Hanseatic city at the Vistula mouth, was the grain staple of the 'mother trade' — the outlet through which the Polish-Lithuanian breadbasket fed Amsterdam and the west.
doc: Danzig (Gdańsk) lies at the mouth of the Vistula, and its wealth was the river: the grain of the vast Polish-Lithuanian interior was floated down to its granaries and shipped out through the Sound to Amsterdam, making it the pivot of what contemporaries and historians alike called the 'mother trade' in Baltic corn. Beside rye and wheat it exported timber, potash, pitch, tar and other naval stores, and it did so as a largely self-governing city that flew its own colours while acknowledging the suzerainty of the Polish crown — the reason the chart files it under the Hanse carrier flag rather than a sovereign one.

Danzig's zenith was roughly 1590 to 1650, when it was among the busiest ports of northern Europe; the mid-seventeenth-century wars, the Swedish 'deluge' in Poland, and the long decline of Polish grain exports gradually eroded its primacy, and in the Second Partition of 1793 the city was annexed by Prussia. Because the dataset offers no Polish-Lithuanian or Prussian sovereign flag, that constitutional history is smeared under the Hanse flag here and its true suzerain is logged as a declared silence.
sources: Maria Bogucka, Baltic Commerce and Urban Society, 1500–1700: Gdańsk/Danzig and its Polish Context (2003)
Milja van Tielhof, The Mother of All Trades: The Baltic Grain Trade in Amsterdam from the Late Sixteenth to the Early Nineteenth Century (2002)

blurb.text: Riga, the Hanseatic mast and naval-stores port on the Düna, shipped the tall timber, hemp and flax that rigged the fleets of western Europe.
Under the Swedish crown after 1621, Riga was the largest city of the Swedish empire and its chief exporter of masts, hemp and flax.
Taken by Russia in 1710, Riga remained the tsar's principal Baltic outlet for timber, hemp and flax.

doc: Riga sits where the Düna (Daugava) empties into the gulf that bears the city's name, and its trade was floated to it: hemp, flax and above all the tall mast-timber of the Russian and Lithuanian interior came down the river to be sold to the naval powers of the west, for whom Riga masts and Riga hemp were strategic materials. The harbour lay a little upriver of the exposed gulf, and its exports of cordage stock and ship-timber made it one of the indispensable naval-stores ports of the Baltic.

Its sovereignty changed hands three times across the era: a Hanseatic city under a fading order and then Polish-Lithuanian suzerainty in the sixteenth century, conquered by Sweden in 1621 (whereupon it became the largest city of the Swedish empire), and taken by Russia in 1710 during the Great Northern War, a possession confirmed in 1721. The chart tiles those rulers as far as its power set allows — the Hanse flag for the pre-Swedish period, then Sweden, then Russia — and logs the missing Polish-Lithuanian sovereign as a declared silence.
sources: David Kirby, The Baltic World 1492–1772 (1990)

stockholm.blurb: Stockholm, at the outlet of Lake Mälaren, was the Swedish crown's naval base and iron staple, exporting the bar iron that dominated Europe's markets.
doc: Stockholm guards the narrow passage between Lake Mälaren and the Baltic, a naval and mercantile capital hemmed in by its archipelago. By the 'Bothnian trade compulsion' the crown forced the exports of northern Sweden and Finland to pass through the capital (and a few privileged staple towns), and the great commodity that passed was iron — osmund and, increasingly, high-quality bar iron — together with tar, pitch and timber. The city was also the base of the Swedish battle fleet.

Swedish bar iron was the premium grade on the European market through the seventeenth and much of the eighteenth century, and Stockholm's merchant houses and the crown's fiscal and naval apparatus rose and fell with it; British demand for Swedish iron, and later competition from Russian and British iron, shaped the port's fortunes across the era.
sources: David Kirby, The Baltic World 1492–1772 (1990)

copenhagen.blurb: Copenhagen, commanding the Øresund where Denmark levied the Sound Toll on all Baltic traffic, was a royal capital, naval base and neutral entrepot.
doc: Copenhagen lies on the Øresund, the narrow strait that is the main gate between the Baltic and the North Sea, and Danish power over that gate was the Sound Toll — levied a little to the north at Elsinore under the guns of Kronborg on every ship that passed, the registers of which are among the great serial records of European trade. The city itself was the Danish-Norwegian capital, the base of the royal fleet, and a well-sheltered staple harbour.

Copenhagen was the entrepot of the Danish colonial ventures — the Asiatic Company's Canton and Tranquebar trade and the Danish West Indies — and, above all, a neutral carrier: its 'flourishing period' in the late eighteenth century turned on Danish neutrality while the great powers fought. That prosperity ended abruptly with the British bombardment of the city and seizure of the fleet in 1807.
sources: David Kirby, The Baltic World 1492–1772 (1990)
Nina Ellinger Bang, Tabeller over Skibsfart og Varetransport gennem Øresund 1497–1660 (1906–1922)

st-petersburg.blurb: St Petersburg, founded by Peter the Great in 1703 at the Neva mouth, became Russia's 'window on the West' and its chief exporter of hemp, flax, iron and naval stores.
doc: St Petersburg was willed into being by Peter the Great in 1703 on the marshy delta of the Neva at the head of the Gulf of Finland, at once a new capital, a naval arsenal and Russia's deliberate 'window on the West'; the naval station of Kronstadt on its island guarded the shallow approach, over whose bar large ships had to be lightened. Because the port did not exist before 1703, the chart holds its dot absent until that year.

It grew with remarkable speed into Russia's principal export harbour, shipping the hemp, flax, bar iron, timber and other naval stores on which the British and Dutch navies depended — a trade the state channelled to the new city partly by restricting the older northern outlet at Arkhangelsk. Through the eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries its foreign-going traffic, much of it in British bottoms, made it one of the busiest ports of the Baltic.
sources: Arcadius Kahan, The Plow, the Hammer, and the Knout: An Economic History of Eighteenth-Century Russia (1985)
David Kirby, The Baltic World 1492–1772 (1990)

amsterdam.blurb: Amsterdam was the staple market of Europe — the entrepot where Baltic grain and the returns of the VOC and WIC were warehoused and redistributed, and the continent's leading capital market.
doc: Amsterdam stands on the Amstel and the IJ, sheltered deep behind the Zuiderzee, its one weakness the shallow Pampus bar across the approach, over which deep-laden ships had to be lightened or floated across on 'camels'. It was the staple market of Europe: the great warehouse into which flowed Baltic grain and timber, the spices and textiles of the VOC's Asian trade, the sugar and enslaved-labour produce carried by the WIC's Atlantic system, and the goods of every European coast, to be graded, stored and re-exported. It was also the money market of the continent, home of the Wisselbank and the bourse.

The city's rise was sealed when the fall of Antwerp in 1585 drove capital, skills and merchants north; the seventeenth century was its golden age as the hub of world commerce and finance, and the eighteenth a long relative decline as English and other competitors grew, though it remained enormously rich as a capital market. The collapse of the Dutch Republic under French pressure in 1795 broke the old order, and the port revived more modestly under the Kingdom of the Netherlands after 1815.
sources: Jonathan I. Israel, Dutch Primacy in World Trade, 1585–1740 (1989)
Jan de Vries and Ad van der Woude, The First Modern Economy: Success, Failure, and Perseverance of the Dutch Economy, 1500–1815 (1997)

hamburg.blurb: Hamburg, a neutral free imperial city on the lower Elbe, was the entrepot linking the German interior with the Atlantic and Baltic and a great wartime carrier.
doc: Hamburg lies on the lower Elbe about a hundred kilometres from the open sea, a tidal river port and a self-governing free imperial city that guarded its neutrality jealously. Its position made it the natural entrepot between central Germany, reached up the Elbe, and the sea lanes of the Atlantic, the North Sea and the Baltic, handling grain, textiles, and the colonial goods — sugar, coffee, tobacco — that its merchants drew from Iberia, France and the wider Atlantic.

The city's fortune was neutrality and connection: German merchant houses at Cadiz, Bordeaux and elsewhere fed a large Hamburg re-export trade, and the port grew fastest precisely when the maritime powers were at war and needed a neutral carrier. Its one great interruption was the French annexation and Continental-System blockade of 1806–1814, after which it recovered strongly and became one of the leading ports of nineteenth-century Europe.
sources: Klaus Weber, Deutsche Kaufleute im Atlantikhandel 1680–1830: Unternehmen und Familien in Hamburg, Cádiz und Bordeaux (2004)

rotterdam.blurb: Rotterdam, on the Nieuwe Maas in the Rhine–Meuse delta, was the Dutch Republic's second port, its advantage the transit trade up the Rhine into Germany.
doc: Rotterdam sits on the Nieuwe Maas in the Rhine–Meuse delta, with more direct access to both the North Sea and the Rhine hinterland than Amsterdam. It was the second port of the Dutch Republic, seat of a chamber of both the VOC and the WIC, and its particular strength was the transit trade: goods landed at Rotterdam moved up the Rhine into the German interior, and German produce came down to be shipped out.

In the golden age Rotterdam lived in Amsterdam's shadow, but its river position left it well placed as the Rhine transit trade grew, and after the disruptions of the Revolutionary and Napoleonic years it rose steadily toward the great transit port it would become in the industrial nineteenth century. Its English and Scottish merchant connections were an old and important part of its business.
sources: Jonathan I. Israel, Dutch Primacy in World Trade, 1585–1740 (1989)

ostend.blurb: Ostend, a modest Flemish harbour in the Austrian Netherlands, was for a few decades the base of private East India voyages and the chartered Ostend Company's Canton and Bengal trade.
doc: Ostend is a small harbour on the Flemish coast which, after the Peace of Utrecht handed the southern Netherlands to Austria, briefly became the one Habsburg deep-sea outlet on the North Sea. From 1715 private merchants fitted out voyages from it to Canton and to Bengal, and in 1722 these were gathered into a chartered body, the Generale Keizerlijke Indische Compagnie — the Ostend Company — which for a few years carried a genuinely competitive share of the European tea and Bengal-textile trade.

The Company's success alarmed the British and Dutch East India Companies, and under their diplomatic pressure the Emperor suspended and then, by the Treaty of Vienna, wound it up; a Bengal afterlife at the factory of Banquibazar lingered to 1744, which is why the chart clamps Ostend's active window to 1715–1744. Outside that episode Ostend was a minor fishing and privateering port, not a first-rank entrepot.
sources: Gerald B. Hertz, 'England and the Ostend Company', English Historical Review 22 (1907)

bordeaux.blurb: Bordeaux, the deep tidal wine port on the Garonne, was eighteenth-century France's leading colonial entrepot and, among its trades, an outfitter of slaving voyages.
doc: Bordeaux lies on the Garonne above its junction with the Dordogne in the Gironde estuary, a deep tidal river port whose ancient staple was wine — the claret shipped for centuries to England, the Hanse towns and the Dutch. In the eighteenth century it became France's leading colonial port, the great entrepot for the sugar, coffee and indigo of Saint-Domingue and the other French islands, much of it refined or re-exported onward to northern Europe by a wealthy merchant community that included many foreign houses.

Bordeaux merchants also armed vessels for the traite négrière, the trade in enslaved Africans carried from the West African coast to the French Antilles; it fitted out fewer such expeditions than Nantes, but the traffic — the forced transport of people against their will — was part of its colonial commerce and is recorded here plainly, not as an item of profit. The port's prosperity peaked in the decades before 1789 and was broken by the Revolution, the maritime wars, and the loss of Saint-Domingue in the Haitian Revolution, recovering only partially in the nineteenth century.
sources: Paul Butel, Les négociants bordelais, l'Europe et les îles au XVIIIe siècle (1974)
Robert Louis Stein, The French Slave Trade in the Eighteenth Century: An Old Regime Business (1979)

nantes.blurb: Nantes, on the Loire estuary, was France's chief slave-trading port — outfitting the largest share of the traite négrière, the forced transport of enslaved Africans to the French Antilles.
doc: Nantes lies on the tidal Loire a little above its estuary, and in the eighteenth century it was the principal slave-trading port of France: its merchants outfitted the largest share of French expeditions of the traite négrière, carrying enslaved Africans from the Bight of Benin, the Bight of Biafra and Angola to the French sugar islands, above all Saint-Domingue, and returning with colonial produce. This was the forced transport of people against their will, and it is recorded here as fact, without any framing of value or gain. The estuary's shifting shoals meant much of the largest shipping used the outports downriver, Paimbœuf and later Saint-Nazaire.

Around the slaving voyages grew a colonial economy of sugar refining and re-export that made Nantes rich in the decades from the 1760s to 1792. The Revolution and the loss of Saint-Domingue broke the legal trade, though clandestine slaving voyages from the port continued into the 1820s and 1830s in defiance of abolition; thereafter Nantes turned to other colonial and industrial commerce as its river increasingly favoured the newer port downstream.
sources: Jean Meyer, L'armement nantais dans la deuxième moitié du XVIIIe siècle (1969)
Robert Louis Stein, The French Slave Trade in the Eighteenth Century: An Old Regime Business (1979)

blurb.text: Built on the sand spit at the mouth of Jamaica's finest harbour, Port Royal was England's Caribbean stronghold, privateer base, and slave mart until an earthquake dropped much of it into the sea in 1692.

Laid out across the bay after the earthquake, Kingston became the British West Indies' greatest entrepot — sugar and rum outward, enslaved Africans landed and sold onward to the Spanish Main — and the base of the Royal Navy's Jamaica Station.

doc: Jamaica's south coast offers one of the finest harbours in the Caribbean: a wide sheltered basin behind the Palisadoes, a long sand spit whose tip carried the town of Port Royal and whose landward shore, across the bay, later carried Kingston. The English took the island from Spain in 1655, and Port Royal became their commercial and naval stronghold — a market for sugar and rum, a base for privateers, and, through the asiento and the open slave trade, a place where enslaved Africans were landed and sold on to the Spanish Main. When a 1692 earthquake dropped much of Port Royal into the sea, the merchants moved across the water to the new grid-planned town of Kingston.

Through the eighteenth century Kingston grew into the largest British port in the Caribbean and one of the busiest slave markets in the Americas, re-exporting captives across the region while shipping the sugar and rum of Jamaica's plantations home to Britain. It was also the headquarters of the Royal Navy's Jamaica Station, guarding the trade and the approaches to the Spanish colonies. The town remained the island's commercial heart across the era, outlasting the abolition of the slave trade in 1807 and emancipation in 1834, though the plantation economy that had built it declined thereafter.
sources: Nuala Zahedieh, 'The Merchants of Port Royal, Jamaica, and the Spanish Contraband Trade, 1655–1692,' William and Mary Quarterly (1986).
Trevor Burnard, Mastery, Tyranny, and Desire: Thomas Thistlewood and His Slaves in the Anglo-Jamaican World (2004).

havana.blurb: Reached through a narrow channel under the guns of El Morro, Havana was the rendezvous of the Spanish treasure fleets and a royal shipyard; after the British occupation of 1762 its sugar economy — and the enslaved labour it rested on — expanded steeply.
doc: Havana's harbour is a deep landlocked pouch entered through a single narrow channel, guarded by the castles of El Morro and La Punta — one of the most defensible and capacious anchorages in the Americas. That geography made it the assembly point of the Spanish Indies: the treasure fleets from Veracruz and the Tierra Firme galleons rendezvoused here to form the annual convoy home, and a royal shipyard grew up to build and repair the fleet, in time launching ships of the line from Cuban hardwoods.

For most of the era Havana lived by the fleets, by fortification, and by tobacco, with sugar a secondary concern. The British captured the city in 1762 and held it for eleven months, opening it to a flood of trade and enslaved Africans before it was returned to Spain in 1763 in exchange for Florida. That opening, and the collapse of French Saint-Domingue after 1791, launched a sugar boom that made Cuba the leading producer in the world by the nineteenth century — an expansion built on a steeply rising traffic in enslaved people, which continued at Havana long after other powers had suppressed it.
sources: Alejandro de la Fuente, Havana and the Atlantic in the Sixteenth Century (2008).
John Robert McNeill, Atlantic Empires of France and Spain: Louisbourg and Havana, 1700–1763 (1985).

veracruz.blurb: New Spain's one legal Atlantic port, an unhealthy roadstead sheltered behind the island fortress of San Juan de Ulúa: silver and cochineal went out to Spain, European goods came in, and yellow fever thinned every crew that lingered.
doc: Veracruz was less a harbour than an open roadstead on a low, sandy, fever-ridden coast, where ships moored behind the island fortress of San Juan de Ulúa — made fast to bronze rings set in its wall — for what shelter the reef and the fort could give against the fierce winter northers. It was nonetheless the sole legal Atlantic port of New Spain: the terminus of the annual flota, through which the silver of the Mexican mines and the cochineal of Oaxaca went out to Spain and European manufactures came in, distributed inland from the great fair later held up-country at Jalapa. The eastern end of the overland link from Acapulco, it was also where the Pacific silver of the Manila trade met the Atlantic.

The port's history across the era is one of strategic importance shadowed by disease: the vómito negro made it deadly to newcomers, and its trade waxed and waned with the fleet system and the eighteenth-century reforms that loosened it. After Mexico's independence, proclaimed in 1821, Veracruz passed to the new nation, though a Spanish garrison held out in San Juan de Ulúa until 1825 as the last royalist foothold on the mainland; in this chart the flag over the port is carried unchanged, as it is for the other ports of New Spain. It remained Mexico's principal Gulf gateway thereafter.
sources: Antonio García de León, Tierra adentro, mar en fuera: El puerto de Veracruz y su litoral a sotavento, 1519–1821 (2011).
John Robert McNeill, Mosquito Empires: Ecology and War in the Greater Caribbean, 1620–1914 (2010).

cartagena.blurb: The most heavily fortified harbour in Spanish America and one of the three licensed slave ports of the Indies, Cartagena landed thousands of enslaved Africans for sale into the interior and sheltered the Tierra Firme galleons bound for the Portobelo fair.
doc: Cartagena de Indias sits behind a large bay screened from the open sea by islands and spits, its entrances narrowed and its landward side walled and bastioned into the strongest fortress-port in Spanish America. It was the Caribbean terminus of the Tierra Firme galleons and, with Veracruz and Portobelo, one of only three ports licensed to land enslaved Africans in the Spanish Indies — the principal legal disembarkation point for the whole of Spanish South America, from which captives were sold on into New Granada, Peru, and beyond. Hides, tobacco, and the emeralds and gold of the interior went out in return.

Its wealth and strategic weight made it a target: Francis Drake sacked the city in 1586, and a great British expedition under Admiral Vernon failed against its defences in 1741. As the fair-and-galleon system decayed through the eighteenth century the port's commercial role narrowed, though its fortifications kept it central to Spanish imperial defence. Cartagena declared its independence in 1811 and endured a punishing royalist siege under Morillo in 1815, passing with the rest of New Granada out of Spanish rule.
sources: David Wheat, Atlantic Africa and the Spanish Caribbean, 1570–1640 (2016).
Lance R. Grahn, The Political Economy of Smuggling: Regional Informal Economies in Early Bourbon New Granada (1997).

portobelo.blurb: A pestilential little bay on the isthmus that woke once or twice a decade for the great fair, when the Tierra Firme galleons met the mule trains bringing Peruvian silver over from Panama; Admiral Vernon's raid of 1739 helped finish both the fair and the fleet system.
doc: Portobelo is a small, deep, well-sheltered bay on the Caribbean side of the Panama isthmus, ringed by forts but pestilential and nearly empty for most of the year. Its purpose was the fair: when the Tierra Firme galleons arrived, the silver of Peru — carried by sea up to Panama on the Pacific side and then over the isthmus by mule train — was exchanged there for European manufactures in one of the great commercial gatherings of the Spanish empire. Like Cartagena, it was also a licensed port for landing enslaved Africans.

The fair made Portobelo the Caribbean hinge of a Pacific empire, but it was a fragile role: disease, the difficulty of the crossing, and the growth of contraband steadily undermined it. Admiral Vernon captured and demolished the forts in 1739, and the galleon-and-fair system was abandoned soon after in favour of individual register ships sailing round Cape Horn. The town dwindled to a decayed garrison, its great fortifications standing over an all-but-empty bay for the remainder of the era.
sources: Christopher Ward, Imperial Panama: Commerce and Conflict in Isthmian America, 1550–1800 (1993).
Geoffrey J. Walker, Spanish Politics and Imperial Trade, 1700–1789 (1979).

bridgetown.blurb: The windward landfall of the Atlantic crossing and England's first great sugar island: from the 1640s Bridgetown shipped sugar from plantations worked by enslaved Africans, and Carlisle Bay's open roadstead was crowded with the ships that carried both.
doc: Barbados is the most windward of the Antilles, the first land a ship reached running before the Atlantic trade winds, and Bridgetown grew at Carlisle Bay — an open roadstead rather than an enclosed harbour, with a small careening creek at its heart. English settlers arrived in 1627, and within two decades the island underwent the 'sugar revolution' that made it the earliest and, for a time, the richest of the English plantation colonies. Sugar went out to Britain; enslaved Africans were landed in large numbers to work the estates, and Barbados became both a model of the plantation-slave society and a redistribution point for enslaved people across the eastern Caribbean.

Through the later seventeenth century Bridgetown was the busiest English port in the Americas, its roadstead crowded with the ships of the sugar and slave trades and its wharves the commercial heart of the English West Indies. As Jamaica and the Leeward Islands developed, the island lost its primacy, but it remained a major commercial and naval station and the administrative centre of the eastern Caribbean. Its plantation economy persisted to emancipation in 1834 and beyond, long after the early wealth had passed its peak.
sources: Richard S. Dunn, Sugar and Slaves: The Rise of the Planter Class in the English West Indies, 1624–1713 (1972).
Hilary McD. Beckles, A History of Barbados: From Amerindian Settlement to Nation-State (1990).

blurb.text: The commercial capital of Saint-Domingue, the wealthiest of all the plantation colonies, resting on the sugar and coffee of the northern plain and on the enslaved Africans landed here in greater numbers than at any other French port; the general uprising of 1791 began the revolution that would end it.

After the enslaved population overthrew the plantation regime and declared independent Haiti in 1804, the burned town was rebuilt as Cap-Haïtien — for a time Cap-Henri, capital of Henri Christophe's northern kingdom.

doc: Cap-Français lay on a reef-sheltered bay on the north coast of Saint-Domingue, a planned grid-town so prosperous it was called the Paris of the Antilles. It was the commercial capital of what became, in the eighteenth century, the single most valuable colony in the world: the sugar, coffee, and indigo of the northern plain flowed out through it, and enslaved Africans were landed here in greater numbers than at any other French colonial port, to be worked — and worked to death — on the surrounding plantations.

That wealth rested entirely on the labour of an enslaved majority, and in August 1791 the enslaved population of the northern plain rose in a revolt that opened the Haitian Revolution. The town was burned in 1793, fought over through the years of French, British, and Spanish intervention, and finally passed, with the declaration of Haitian independence on 1 January 1804, out of French hands altogether. Rebuilt as Cap-Haïtien — and for a decade as Cap-Henri, capital of Henri Christophe's kingdom — it remained the chief town of Haiti's north. (This chart has no separate flag for independent Haiti; the post-1804 window is carried under France as the nearest available marker, which the note records rather than endorses.)
sources: C. L. R. James, The Black Jacobins: Toussaint L'Ouverture and the San Domingo Revolution (1938).
John D. Garrigus, Before Haiti: Race and Citizenship in French Saint-Domingue (2006).

st-eustatius.blurb: A tiny volcanic island with no harbour but a warehouse-lined road, St Eustatius was the 'Golden Rock' — a neutral Dutch free port where every nation's goods, contraband, and enslaved people in transit changed hands, until Admiral Rodney sacked it in 1781 for arming the American rebels.
doc: St Eustatius is a tiny volcanic island with no harbour at all — only an open roadstead on its leeward side, where a mile-long strand below the cliff at Oranjestad was packed with warehouses and ships anchored in the road. Holding almost no plantation land of its own, the island lived entirely as a free port: a neutral Dutch trans-shipment market where the goods of every nation — sugar, tobacco, manufactures, contraband, arms, and enslaved people held for re-export — were bought and sold outside the mercantilist rules of the surrounding empires.

Dutch from 1636 and changing hands more than twenty times, 'the Golden Rock' reached its height in the mid-eighteenth century, and above all as a conduit of munitions to the rebelling North American colonies. For that trade a British fleet under Admiral Rodney seized and plundered the island in 1781, an act from which its commerce never fully recovered. It reverted to the Dutch but declined through the remaining decades of the era as the free-port trade drifted to neutral rivals.
sources: Wim Klooster, Illicit Riches: Dutch Trade in the Caribbean, 1648–1795 (1998).
J. Franklin Jameson, 'St. Eustatius in the American Revolution,' American Historical Review (1903).

curacao.blurb: Willemstad's enclosed harbour made Curaçao the Dutch West India Company's Caribbean base and, under the asiento, a depot where enslaved Africans were landed and held for re-export to the Spanish Main, alongside a heavy contraband trade with the surrounding coasts.
doc: Curaçao possesses one of the best harbours in the Caribbean — the Schottegat, a large enclosed lagoon reached through the narrow Sint Anna Bay channel at Willemstad — on an island too dry for plantation agriculture. That combination made it a commercial rather than a planting colony: the Dutch West India Company's Caribbean headquarters and entrepôt, and, under the asiento contracts, a depot where enslaved Africans were landed and held before being re-exported to the Spanish Main at Cartagena and along the Venezuelan coast.

Through the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries Willemstad thrived on this trans-shipment trade and on a large contraband commerce with the surrounding Spanish colonies, dealing in salt, dyewood, and manufactures as well as in human beings. Fortified and repeatedly threatened but seldom taken, it remained the principal Dutch base in the region across the era, its fortunes shifting with the asiento and with the wider wars but never displaced from its role as the Dutch commercial hinge of the southern Caribbean.
sources: Linda M. Rupert, Creolization and Contraband: Curaçao in the Early Modern Atlantic World (2012).
Wim Klooster, Illicit Riches: Dutch Trade in the Caribbean, 1648–1795 (1998).

st-thomas.blurb: A deep sheltered harbour on a dry Danish island, thin until the free-port declaration of 1764 turned Charlotte Amalie into a neutral entrepôt that traded — goods, contraband, and enslaved people in transit — straight through the wars of its larger neighbours.
doc: St Thomas has a deep, hill-ringed, sheltered harbour — among the finest in the Lesser Antilles — on an island too dry and broken for much sugar. Danish from 1672, it was at first a thin plantation venture, its harbour leased for a time to the Brandenburg slave-trading company, and it never became a major producer in its own right. Its fortune lay instead in its position and its neutrality.

The free-port declaration of 1764 turned Charlotte Amalie into a neutral entrepôt that could trade through the wars of its larger neighbours, dealing in trans-shipped produce, contraband, provisions, and enslaved people carried on for sale elsewhere. Through the revolutionary and Napoleonic wars — interrupted by British occupations in 1801 and 1807–15 — it boomed as one of the busiest commercial harbours in the Caribbean, a Danish neutral port living off everyone else's belligerence.
sources: Neville A. T. Hall, Slave Society in the Danish West Indies: St. Thomas, St. John, and St. Croix (1992).
Waldemar Westergaard, The Danish West Indies under Company Rule, 1671–1754 (1917).

paramaribo.blurb: The capital of Dutch Suriname, a river port fifteen miles up from the sea serving a plantation colony of sugar, coffee, and cotton worked — under a regime notorious for its severity — by enslaved Africans, whose flight into the forest built the maroon communities of the interior.
doc: Paramaribo stands on the Suriname River some fifteen miles above its mouth, deep enough for ocean-going ships, with the plantation districts spreading up the rivers behind it. It was the capital of Dutch Suriname, a plantation colony of sugar and, increasingly, coffee, cacao, and cotton, whose produce came down the rivers to be shipped from the town. The estates were worked by enslaved Africans under a regime remembered for its exceptional severity and mortality, which required constant new importation and drove a steady flight of the enslaved into the forest.

The Dutch took the colony from the English in 1667 and kept it — confirmed at the Treaty of Breda, where England retained New Netherland in exchange — and it was administered for much of the era by the chartered Society of Suriname. The eighteenth century saw the plantation economy expand and, with it, sustained warfare against the maroon communities of the interior, who eventually won treaties recognising their freedom. Paramaribo remained the Dutch capital and sole significant port of the colony throughout, its river frontage the outlet for a plantation world built on coerced labour.
sources: Richard Price, The Guiana Maroons: A Historical and Bibliographical Introduction (1976).
Alex van Stipriaan, Surinaams contrast: Roofbouw en overleven in een Caraïbische plantagekolonie, 1750–1863 (1993).

belize.blurb: A cutting settlement, not a plantation colony: from the 1660s English-speaking 'Baymen' worked the Bay of Honduras behind its reef for logwood and, from the 1770s, mahogany — felled by enslaved gangs far up the rivers and rafted down to the Belize River mouth for shipping.
doc: The Bay of Honduras is a low, swampy coast behind a long barrier reef, and the settlement grew at the mouth of the Belize River, where shallow reef-threaded approaches led to an anchorage among the cays. This was never a plantation colony but an extractive one: English-speaking 'Baymen' cut logwood — valued for its dye — from the seventeenth century, and from the 1770s turned to mahogany, felled by enslaved gangs far up the rivers and rafted down to the river mouth to be loaded for export.

Spain claimed the coast and never recognised the cutters' presence, so the settlement lived for most of the era without formal colonial standing, its right to cut wood grudgingly conceded by treaty while its sovereignty stayed contested. A Spanish attempt to clear it was repulsed at the Battle of St George's Caye in 1798. The mahogany trade sustained the settlement into the nineteenth century, and out of the Baymen's foothold grew the colony of British Honduras.
sources: O. Nigel Bolland, The Formation of a Colonial Society: Belize, from Conquest to Crown Colony (1977).
Narda Dobson, A History of Belize (1973).

blurb.text: One of Ming China's great southern ports, trading with Portuguese Macau and the Fujian junk networks.
The sole port open to European trade after 1757, where the hong merchants handled the tea and silk the Canton System channelled through the Thirteen Factories.

doc: Canton (Guangzhou) sat on the Pearl River, its harbour reached from the sea by an ascent past the Bogue forts to the Whampoa anchorage some twelve miles below the city, where the deep-sea ships lay while their business was done at the row of foreign factories crowded onto the waterfront outside the walls. Trade with foreigners was conducted only through a licensed guild of Chinese merchants, the Cohong or hong merchants, who were made personally answerable to the Qing state for the conduct and debts of the barbarian traders; the East India Company, and later Continental companies and the Americans, dealt with these houses and were confined to the factory strip and the trading season.

The dominant export was tea, bought for silver and, increasingly, for the goods of the country trade — Indian raw cotton and, from the later eighteenth century, opium carried from Bengal by private British and other traders to be sold clandestinely for the silver that financed the tea. In 1757 the Qianlong emperor confined all Western maritime trade to Canton alone, making it the single legal gateway between China and the maritime West; the arrangement, the 'Canton System', channelled a vast and lucrative commerce for eight decades until the crisis over the opium traffic brought the war of 1839–42 and the Treaty of Nanjing, which abolished the hong monopoly and opened the treaty ports.
sources: Paul A. Van Dyke, The Canton Trade: Life and Enterprise on the China Coast, 1700-1845 (2005)
Weng Eang Cheong, The Hong Merchants of Canton: Chinese Merchants in Sino-Western Trade (1997)

amoy.blurb: The great Fujian junk port of the Nanyang trade, whose ships carried Hokkien emigrants and Chinese goods across maritime Southeast Asia.
doc: Amoy (Xiamen) lay on an island in a deep, sheltered channel on the southern Fujian coast, facing Taiwan across the strait. Poor in farmland but rich in seafaring, its hinterland sent men to sea rather than to the fields, and the port became the hearth of the private Chinese junk trade to the Nanyang — the Southeast Asian world of Manila, Batavia, Siam, and the Malay ports — where the Hokkien merchant diaspora settled and prospered. In the seventeenth century it was the base of the Zheng maritime power under Koxinga, which held the coast against the new Qing dynasty until 1683.

After the Qing conquest of Taiwan the coast was reopened, and from 1685 Amoy was one of the customs ports where European ships might call, though the confinement of Western trade to Canton in 1757 left it to the junk trade it had always served. Its business was the carrying trade of the South China Sea: Chinese silks, porcelain, and manufactures out; pepper, tin, rice, and tropical produce home; and above all the movement of people, the Fujianese emigration that seeded Chinese communities across the archipelago. This traffic — a Chinese-owned and Chinese-crewed commerce sailing under no European flag — is carried here under the junk-trade allegiance rather than the Qing state, which taxed it but did not sail it.
sources: Ng Chin-keong, Trade and Society: The Amoy Network on the China Coast, 1683-1735 (1983)

macau.blurb: The Portuguese enclave at the mouth of the Pearl River, Europe's oldest foothold on the China coast from 1557.
doc: Macau occupied a small peninsula on the western side of the Pearl River estuary, where from about 1557 the Portuguese were permitted to settle and trade on Chinese sufferance, paying ground rent to the Ming authorities and remaining always under ultimate Chinese jurisdiction. Its inner harbour and roadstead made it the one place Europeans could reside on the China coast, and for its first century it grew rich as the indispensable middleman of a trade Chinese law otherwise forbade: the Portuguese 'Great Ship' carried Chinese silk to Nagasaki and returned with Japanese silver, taking a share of the exchange that neither side would conduct directly.

The closing of Japan to the Portuguese in 1639 cut away that trade and began a long decline into dependence on the Canton commerce, where Macau's chief remaining value was as the place the foreign traders and their families wintered when the Canton factories closed for the off-season, and where the Catholic missions to China were based. Never more than a leased enclave, it kept its Portuguese flag and its usefulness through the whole age of sail while the great volume of the China trade passed it by for Whampoa and Canton upriver, and later for Hong Kong across the estuary.
sources: George Bryan Souza, The Survival of Empire: Portuguese Trade and Society in China and the South China Sea, 1630-1754 (1986)

blurb.text: A busy port of the Ming cotton country in the Yangtze delta, at the heart of the coastal junk trade.
The great junk port of the Jiangnan coastal trade, made a treaty port open to foreign commerce in 1842.

doc: Shanghai stood on the Huangpu a little above its junction with the Yangtze estuary, giving it a sheltered river anchorage with access both to the ocean and, up the great river, to the interior of China. It was the maritime outlet of Jiangnan, the richest and most productive region of the empire, and the fleets of flat-bottomed sand-junks (sha-chuan) that worked from it carried the raw cotton, cloth, and grain of the delta north along the coast to the Shandong and Bohai ports and south to Fujian and Canton. This was a Chinese domestic commerce of a scale the European trading record scarcely registered, since almost none of it passed through foreign hands.

Because the volume of this coastal traffic is poorly counted in the surviving Western sources, its representation here is a reconstruction of a trade known to have been very large rather than a figure read from a ledger — an entry against the silent zero that the Euro-centric record would otherwise leave. The Treaty of Nanjing opened Shanghai to foreign residence and trade in 1842, and within a generation the foreign settlements on the Huangpu made it China's greatest treaty port; but for most of the period charted here it was already a major harbour, its importance domestic and its shipping Chinese.
sources: Linda Cooke Johnson, Shanghai: From Market Town to Treaty Port, 1074-1858 (1995)

blurb.text: The port at the Hai river bar serving the grain supply of the Ming northern capital.
The northern terminus of the coastal grain fleets that fed Peking, at the Dagu bar on the Hai river.
