doc: The seaward approach to Tianjin lay across the bar of the Hai river at Dagu, guarded by forts, where sea-going junks discharged for the passage up to the city and on toward the imperial capital at Peking a hundred kilometres inland. Tianjin's significance was as the maritime doorway to the capital region: the point at which the sea-transport of tribute grain (haiyun) from the productive Yangtze delta reached the north, feeding the enormous non-producing population of Peking and the court. Alongside the Grand Canal, on which the empire chiefly relied, this coastal grain fleet was periodically revived — notably in the 1820s when the canal silted — and at such times it was among the largest single movements of shipping anywhere in the era.

Like the wider Chinese coastal trade, this was a domestic commerce carried in Chinese bottoms and administered by the Chinese state, and it is entered here as a large flow reconstructed from the administrative record of the grain transport rather than counted in any foreign source — a deliberate refusal of the silent zero, since a movement of this scale left almost no trace in the European archive. Tianjin did not open to foreign trade within the period charted here; its treaty-port career began only in 1858, and the port on this chart is the grain harbour of Qing China, not a Western one.
sources: Jane Kate Leonard, Controlling from Afar: The Daoguang Emperor's Management of the Grand Canal Crisis, 1824-1826 (1996)

hong-kong.blurb: Ceded to Britain in 1841 during the first Opium War; a deep-water harbour founded from the start as a free port and colony.
doc: Hong Kong was taken for its harbour and for nothing else. The deep, sheltered anchorage between the mountainous island and the Kowloon peninsula could hold a fleet in any weather, and it had almost no hinterland and no existing trade — which was precisely the point, for the British occupied it in 1841, and held it by the cession of the first Opium War, as a secure base beyond Qing jurisdiction from which the China trade could be conducted on their own terms. The opium traders who had worked from the Lintin anchorage and from Macau moved their business there, and it became a free port from the outset.

The commerce that founded the colony was in large part the opium traffic that the war had been fought to protect — the drug carried from British India to be sold along the China coast — and the honest record names it as such rather than as an unnamed 'country trade'. Within a few years of its founding Hong Kong was drawing the shipping of the coast into its harbour and beginning the growth that would make it one of the great entrepôts of Asia, but in the years covered by this chart it is a very new place: a naval and mercantile base at the mouth of the Pearl River, a decade old.
sources: John M. Carroll, A Concise History of Hong Kong (2007)
Steve Tsang, A Modern History of Hong Kong (2004)

naha.blurb: The port of the Ryukyu Kingdom, the licensed conduit for tribute trade between China and Japan.
doc: Naha was the harbour of the Ryukyu Kingdom, on the western shore of Okinawa, and the point of departure for the tribute missions that were the kingdom's reason for existing as a trading state. Ryukyu held a formal tributary relationship with Ming and then Qing China, and its licensed ships sailed to Fuzhou to exchange embassies and goods; from this position the tiny kingdom made itself the indispensable middleman of an East Asian trade its neighbours would not conduct openly, moving Chinese silks, medicines, and porcelain against Japanese silver, copper, and marine products, and re-exporting the goods of Southeast Asia it had gathered in its fifteenth- and sixteenth-century heyday as an entrepôt.

After the Satsuma domain of southern Japan invaded and subjugated the kingdom in 1609, Ryukyu was held in a double subordination — tributary to China, controlled by Satsuma — and its China trade was quietly turned to Japanese profit, the Ryukyu conduit laundering an exchange that Japan's own seclusion policy otherwise forbade. The kingdom kept its own flag and its own court through the whole period, and Naha's licensed tribute trade continued, though the great entrepôt days were long past as Chinese junks and European shipping carried directly the trade Ryukyu had once brokered.
sources: Gregory Smits, Visions of Ryukyu: Identity and Ideology in Early-Modern Thought and Politics (1999)
George H. Kerr, Okinawa: The History of an Island People (1958)

busan.blurb: The Japan House (Waegwan) at Busan, the single licensed gateway for Korea's trade with Tokugawa Japan.
doc: Busan, on Korea's south-eastern coast facing the strait toward Japan, was the site of the Waegwan — the Japan House — a walled compound at Choryang where all licensed trade between Joseon Korea and Tokugawa Japan was conducted under close Korean supervision. Japanese residence and commerce in Korea were confined to this enclave, which was staffed and run by the men of the Tsushima domain acting as the Tokugawa shogunate's authorised intermediary; Korean officials controlled access, fixed the terms, and permitted the exchange to proceed within strict bounds, so that the two secluded states could trade without direct contact.

Through the Waegwan passed Korean ginseng and ramie cloth and, above all, the Chinese raw silk that Korea re-exported, going out to Japan; Japanese silver and copper came in, the silver in particular financing Korea's own purchases from Qing China in a triangular exchange. The trade was at its height in the later seventeenth century and declined through the eighteenth as Japan restricted its silver exports and the Chinese silk trade found other channels, but the Japan House remained the fixed, ceremonious point of contact between the two kingdoms until the opening of Korea in the later nineteenth century.
sources: James B. Lewis, Frontier Contact Between Choson Korea and Tokugawa Japan (2003)

tsushima.blurb: The Sō domain's island, the licensed intermediary of the Korea-Japan trade in both directions.
doc: Tsushima is a mountainous island set in the strait between Korea and Kyushu, nearer to Busan than to the Japanese mainland and too poor in arable land to feed itself. Its rulers, the Sō daimyō, lived by the trade the island's position commanded: the Tokugawa shogunate granted the Sō a monopoly over all relations with Joseon Korea, and from this the domain drew its living, running the Japan House at Busan, forwarding Korean and Chinese goods to Japan, and carrying Japanese silver and copper the other way. It was, in effect, a small state whose economy was a trading privilege.

The Sō also managed the diplomatic side of the relationship — arranging and escorting the grand Korean embassies to the shogun's court, and, when it suited both governments, quietly adjusting the documents that passed between two rulers who would not otherwise deal with each other. The domain's fortunes rose and fell with the trade it brokered, flourishing while Japanese silver flowed and Chinese silk moved through Busan, and straitening as those exports were cut back in the eighteenth century; but the licensed intermediary role, and with it Tsushima's peculiar importance, lasted until the Meiji Restoration remade Japan's foreign relations.
sources: James B. Lewis, Frontier Contact Between Choson Korea and Tokugawa Japan (2003)
Robert I. Hellyer, Defining Engagement: Japan and Global Contexts, 1640-1868 (2009)

ayutthaya.blurb: The cosmopolitan capital of Siam, whose crown junks and foreign factories traded across the China and Japan seas until the city's fall in 1767.
doc: Ayutthaya, the capital of the Siamese kingdom, stood on an island in the Chao Phraya some eighty kilometres from the sea; ocean-going ships anchored at the river bar and their cargoes were lightered up to the city, which lay inland yet functioned as one of the great ports of Southeast Asia. It was strikingly cosmopolitan: separate quarters housed Chinese, Japanese, Malay, Cham, Persian, Portuguese, Dutch, and French communities, and the court drew on foreign expertise and foreign merchants alike. Much of the overseas trade was a royal monopoly, the crown operating its own junks to China and Japan and requiring foreign traders to deal through its warehouses; the exports were deerskins and sappanwood to Japan, tin, rice, and forest produce, and the manufactures and silver of the wider trade in return.

The port reached its height under King Narai in the later seventeenth century, when Persian and then French influence at court was strong; the French bid for position ended abruptly in the revolution of 1688 that expelled them, after which Siam turned somewhat inward while its China trade, increasingly carried by resident Chinese, continued to grow. Ayutthaya's long history as a trading capital was ended in 1767, when a Burmese army took and destroyed the city — the event that closes its window on this chart, the Siamese state afterward rebuilding its port downriver toward Bangkok.
sources: Chris Baker and Pasuk Phongpaichit, A History of Ayutthaya: Siam in the Early Modern World (2017)

blurb.text: The Danish factory on the Coromandel coast, Fort Dansborg, trading Indian textiles under a neutral flag.
Sold to Britain in 1845, ending two and a quarter centuries of Danish India.

doc: Tranquebar (Tharangambadi) was a small Danish enclave on the Coromandel coast of south-eastern India, established in 1620 when the first Danish East India Company obtained a grant from the Nayak of Tanjore and built Fort Dansborg on the open, surf-beaten shore. It was never large — a fort, a town, and a strip of territory — but it gave Denmark a foothold in the Indian trade, and its chief business was the printed and plain cotton textiles of the Coromandel weavers, the staple return cargo of the Asian trade, alongside a share of the pepper and later the China tea that the Danish companies carried home.

The enduring value of the Danish flag lay in its neutrality: in the recurrent wars between Britain, France, and the Dutch, Danish ships from Tranquebar could trade and carry where the belligerents could not, and the reconstituted Danish Asiatic Company profited from this niche, particularly in the tea trade to Canton. The settlement passed its whole life as a modest but persistent enterprise on a coast dominated by greater European powers, until in 1845 the Danish crown sold Tranquebar and its other Indian possessions to the British East India Company, ending the Danish presence in India.
sources: Ole Feldbaek, India Trade Under the Danish Flag, 1772-1808 (1969)

blurb.text: Mughal India's greatest port, home of the Gujarati merchant fleet and the pilgrim and trading ships to the Red Sea.
Under East India Company control after 1759, declining as its trade drained away to Bombay.

doc: Surat lay on the Tapti a short way above its mouth in Gujarat, the sea-going ships anchoring in the Swally roadstead a few miles off while their goods went up to the city; for a century and a half it was the greatest port of Mughal India. Its wealth was Indian: the Gujarati and Muslim merchant houses of Surat owned a great fleet of their own, and the port's largest ships were the pilgrim and trading vessels that sailed each season to Mocha and Jedda in the Red Sea, carrying Indian cotton textiles to be exchanged for the silver and gold of the Ottoman lands and for the returning pilgrims of the hajj. It was also the point of entry for the European companies — the English established their factory there in 1613, the Dutch soon after — who traded alongside, and in the shadow of, the far larger Indian commerce.

Surat's prosperity rested on the Mughal peace that kept its hinterland and its trade routes secure, and it declined as that peace failed: Maratha raids from the 1660s, the weakening of central authority after 1700, and disorder in its own government eroded the port through the first half of the eighteenth century. The English East India Company took control of the castle and the port's affairs in 1759, but by then the trade was already draining south to the Company's own harbour at Bombay, and Surat subsided into a provincial town, its Mughal sovereign long since nominal and its great merchant fleet dispersed.
sources: Ashin Das Gupta, Indian Merchants and the Decline of Surat, c.1700-1750 (1979)

manila.blurb: Spain's Asian capital, where the silver of the Americas was exchanged for the silks and porcelain of China.
doc: Manila stood on the east bank of the Pasig where the river meets its great bay, a sheltered basin screened from the South China Sea by the island of Corregidor. The Spanish town proper was Intramuros, a walled quadrilateral of stone begun after 1571; across the river and outside the walls lay the parián, the Chinese quarter, where the Sangley merchants who supplied the trade were confined and taxed. Once a year the Manila galleon crossed to Acapulco and back, the single legal artery of a monopoly that tied the Philippines to the Viceroyalty of New Spain rather than to Madrid.

The business of the port was the exchange of Mexican and Peruvian silver, carried west in the galleon, for Chinese silk, porcelain, and lacquer brought down in junks from Fujian and Canton — a trade so lucrative that Manila lived by it and grew little else. The Sangley community that made it possible was also periodically its victim: mass killings of the Chinese in 1603, 1639, 1662, 1686, and 1762 punctuate the era. The city was held for two years by a British expedition after 1762, saw the galleon line dwindle and finally end in 1815, and was opened to foreign merchant shipping in the decades that followed as the old monopoly gave way.
sources: William Lytle Schurz, The Manila Galleon (1939)
Katharine Bjork, "The Link That Kept the Philippines Spanish: Mexican Merchant Interests and the Manila Trade, 1571-1815," Journal of World History (1998)

banda-neira.blurb: The world's sole source of nutmeg and mace, held under a Dutch monopoly imposed by the conquest and near-destruction of the Bandanese in 1621.
doc: The Banda Islands are a cluster of small volcanic specks in the eastern Indonesian sea — Neira, the larger Lonthor (Banda Besar), and the smoking cone of Gunung Api among them — and for most of the era they were the only place on earth where the nutmeg tree grew and bore. Banda Neira's harbour, a narrow strait between two of the islands, was commanded by the Dutch forts Nassau and Belgica; the nutmeg groves behind them, the perken, were the whole economy. There was no hinterland and no other reason for ships to come: the islands mattered because of a single tree.

Before 1621 the islands were governed by their own orang kaya, councils of Bandanese notables who sold nutmeg to Javanese, Malay, Chinese, and eventually English and Dutch buyers as they chose. To convert that open trade into an absolute monopoly, the Dutch East India Company under Jan Pieterszoon Coen conquered Banda in 1621 and destroyed the society that lived there: of a population estimated at around fifteen thousand, the great majority were killed, starved during the fighting, deported, or enslaved, and the islands were repopulated with enslaved and bonded labourers set to work the confiscated groves for the Company. This is recorded here as fact, not as a commercial success. The monopoly held for a century and a half until nutmeg seedlings were smuggled out — to the French Mascarenes in the mid-eighteenth century and by the British to Penang and Ceylon during their wartime occupations — after which Banda's singular value drained away.
sources: Willard A. Hanna, Indonesian Banda: Colonialism and its Aftermath in the Nutmeg Islands (1978)
Vincent C. Loth, "Armed Incidents and Unpaid Bills: Anglo-Dutch Rivalry in the Banda Islands in the Seventeenth Century," Modern Asian Studies 29 (1995)

blurb.text: Gowa's great free port, where any nation's ship could buy the spices the Dutch were trying to monopolise.
After the VOC's conquest of 1669 a garrisoned Dutch town, its free trade scattered into the Bugis diaspora.

doc: Makassar sits on the south-western foot of Sulawesi, on a shallow, reef-fringed roadstead where the twin sultanates of Gowa and Tallo built their port. Through the first half of the era it was the archipelago's great open market: a cosmopolitan town of Malays, Bugis, Javanese, Chinese, and resident Portuguese, Danish, and English traders, where the cloves and nutmeg that the Dutch were struggling to monopolise in Maluku could be bought freely by anyone. Its rulers made a deliberate principle of that openness, holding that God had made the sea free to all.

That policy made Makassar the chief obstacle to the VOC's spice monopoly, and in 1666-69 the Company, allied with the Bugis prince Arung Palakka of Bone, made war on Gowa and took the city; the Treaty of Bungaya and the fall of the fortress in 1669 ended its independence and its free trade. The Dutch garrisoned the rebuilt fort, renamed Fort Rotterdam, and Makassar became a controlled colonial town. Its dispersed traders — above all the Bugis — carried the archipelago's own carrying trade outward from here for the next century and a half, so that the port's commercial energy survived the loss of its sovereignty.
sources: Leonard Y. Andaya, The Heritage of Arung Palakka: A History of South Sulawesi (Celebes) in the Seventeenth Century (1981)
Anthony Reid, Southeast Asia in the Age of Commerce, 1450-1680 (1988-1993)

aceh.blurb: The pepper sultanate at Sumatra's northern tip, shipping direct to the Red Sea and outflanking the Portuguese Cape route.
doc: Aceh occupies the northern point of Sumatra, commanding the western approach to the Strait of Malacca, and its harbour at the mouth of the Aceh river was the seat of a Muslim sultanate that rose in the sixteenth century as a rival to Portuguese Melaka. The port drew pepper from its own territories and from the ports it subdued along both Sumatran coasts, and gathered it for shipment across the Bay of Bengal and the Indian Ocean. At its height under Iskandar Muda in the early seventeenth century the sultanate was one of the strongest naval powers in the region.

Aceh's importance lay in the route it kept open: it sold pepper direct to Muslim shipping bound for the Red Sea, Jedda, and the Ottoman Mediterranean, sustaining the older spice road at exactly the moment the Portuguese and then the Dutch were trying to close it and force the trade around the Cape of Good Hope. English and Dutch factors called here early in the seventeenth century for the same pepper. The sultanate's power waned after Iskandar Muda, and though it remained independent — the Dutch would not conquer it until the wars that began in the 1870s, long after this chart's horizon — its commanding role in the pepper trade had passed to others well before 1700.
sources: Anthony Reid, An Indonesian Frontier: Acehnese and Other Histories of Sumatra (2005)
Denys Lombard, Le sultanat d'Atjeh au temps d'Iskandar Muda, 1607-1636 (1967)

bantam.blurb: The archipelago's great pepper emporium and Batavia's rival, until the VOC conquest of 1682-84 ended its independence.
doc: Bantam (Banten Lama) lay on the north-western shoulder of Java, on a low, silting bay guarding the Sunda Strait between Java and Sumatra. It was the pepper emporium of the western archipelago, a large and genuinely international market town where Chinese, Gujarati, Arab, and Malay merchants gathered the pepper of Sumatra and west Java; the first Dutch fleet reached it in 1596 and both the Dutch and the English planted their earliest Java factories here before either had a capital of its own.

That made Bantam the direct commercial rival of the VOC's new headquarters at Batavia, only some eighty kilometres east along the coast, and the two competed for the archipelago's trade for the better part of a century. The rivalry ended by force: the Company intervened in a Bantenese succession dispute and in 1682-84 stormed the town, reducing the sultanate to a Dutch protectorate and diverting its trade to Batavia. The port declined quickly thereafter, its independent commerce extinguished and its harbour left to silt, so that within a generation the once-great emporium had faded from the sea lanes.
sources: Claude Guillot, The Sultanate of Banten (1990)
M. C. Ricklefs, A History of Modern Indonesia since c.1200 (2001)

singapore.blurb: A free port from its founding in 1819, within twenty years the entrepot of the Straits.
doc: Singapore occupies a low island at the southern tip of the Malay Peninsula, on the strait between the peninsula and the Riau archipelago, with a sheltered anchorage at the mouth of the Singapore river. When Stamford Raffles established a British post here in 1819 the island held only a small Malay and Orang Laut settlement under a Temenggong; the site was chosen for its position astride the passage between the Indian Ocean and the South China Sea rather than for any existing town or hinterland.

Raffles declared it a free port from the outset, levying no duties, and that policy drew trade with remarkable speed. The junks of the China trade, the prahus of the archipelago, and the country ships of India converged on it, and within two decades Singapore had displaced older Dutch and Malay ports as the entrepot of the whole Straits region, its population and shipping swelling with Chinese, Malay, Bugis, and Indian merchants. By the 1840s it was the commercial hinge of British Southeast Asia, a role it would only consolidate in the era of steam that followed this chart's close.
sources: C. M. Turnbull, A History of Modern Singapore, 1819-2005 (2009)
Wong Lin Ken, "The Trade of Singapore, 1819-69," Journal of the Malayan Branch of the Royal Asiatic Society 33 (1960)

blurb.text: The port of Lima and base of the South Sea fleet, through which Peruvian silver left for Panama and Spain.
After its fall in 1826, the Pacific port of independent Peru, opening to the world's shipping.

doc: Callao is the port of Lima, a few miles from the viceregal capital on a low, sheltered stretch of the central Peruvian coast protected by the island of San Lorenzo. For two and a half centuries it was the principal Spanish naval and commercial base on the Pacific coast of South America and the home of the Armada del Mar del Sur, the South Sea fleet. Its whole reason for being was the silver of the Andes: bullion from Potosi and the other mountain mines was carried down to Lima and shipped north from Callao to Panama, there to cross the isthmus for the Atlantic convoys and Spain.

The wealth that passed through the port rested on coerced labour, and this is recorded plainly: the silver was dug under the mita, the rotational forced-labour draft that sent Andean communities into the mines, while Lima and its port were also worked in part by enslaved Africans brought across the Pacific and up from the Atlantic ports. Callao was fortified against Dutch and English raiders, wrecked by the great earthquake and tsunami of 1746, and rebuilt as the fortress of Real Felipe. It was the last royalist position to hold out in South America, surrendering in January 1826, after which it became the seaward gate of the independent Peruvian republic and opened to the merchant flags of the world.
sources: Kenneth J. Andrien, Crisis and Decline: The Viceroyalty of Peru in the Seventeenth Century (1985)
Peter Bakewell, Miners of the Red Mountain: Indian Labor in Potosi, 1545-1650 (1984)

blurb.text: The South Sea fleet's shipyard and, after the trade reopened in 1774, a booming cacao port.
After independence a cacao port of Gran Colombia and then Ecuador, shipping to the wider Pacific.

doc: Guayaquil lies up the Guayas river estuary on the Pacific coast of what is now Ecuador, in a hot, timber-rich lowland behind a broad tidal delta. Its abundant hardwoods and its sheltered river berths made it the great shipyard of the Spanish Pacific: through the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries much of the tonnage that sailed the South Sea fleet and the coasting trade was built and careened here, the caulkers and carpenters of colonial Guayaquil supplying hulls that Spanish shipwrights on the drier coasts could not.

The town's other staple was cacao, grown in the estuary lowlands, and when the crown relaxed the old restrictions on colonial trade in 1774 the cacao boom carried Guayaquil into a long rise that ran on across the turn of the century. It declared independence in 1820, joined Gran Colombia in 1822, and passed to the new republic of Ecuador when that state formed in 1830 — a lineage for which this chart has no separate flag, so the post-independence period is marked with the nearest roster power and noted as a proxy. Through those changes of sovereignty the cacao trade held, and by the 1840s Guayaquil was among the busier commercial ports of the eastern Pacific.
sources: Lawrence A. Clayton, Caulkers and Carpenters in a New World: The Shipyards of Colonial Guayaquil (1980)
Maria Luisa Laviana Cuetos, Guayaquil en el siglo XVIII: recursos naturales y desarrollo economico (1987)

blurb.text: The Pacific mouth of the isthmus, where Peruvian silver was landed for the mule road to Portobelo.
After independence a quiet isthmian port of New Granada, its old fair long gone.

doc: The anchorage of Perico lay among the small islands off Panama City in the Gulf of Panama, at the Pacific end of the narrow isthmus that Spanish America crossed by mule. It was less a trading town in its own right than the seaward hinge of a portage: the silver of Peru, shipped up from Callao, was landed on the Pacific side and carried overland to Portobelo on the Caribbean, there to meet the Atlantic galleons at the great isthmus fair. That overland crossing is not modelled here as shipping, which is why the port stands in the chart as a lane endpoint rather than a busy node.

The old Panama City was sacked by Henry Morgan in 1671 and rebuilt on a new site nearby; the Portobelo fair that gave the crossing its purpose ended in 1739, when Spain shifted the South American trade to the Cape Horn route, and the isthmus traffic dwindled thereafter. Panama joined the independence movement in 1821 and became part of Gran Colombia and then New Granada — again a sovereignty for which this chart carries no dedicated flag, so the period is marked with the nearest roster power and noted as a proxy. Through the first half of the nineteenth century it remained a minor port, its great age of transit still awaiting the railway and canal that lay beyond this chart's horizon.
sources: Christopher Ward, Imperial Panama: Commerce and Conflict in Isthmian America, 1550-1800 (1993)
Alfredo Castillero Calvo, Los negros y mulatos libres en la historia social panamena (1969)

blurb.text: Chile's modest Pacific outlet under Spanish rule, an open roadstead below the coastal hills.
After independence the first port of call round the Horn and the rising commercial entrepot of the South Pacific.

doc: Valparaiso is an open bay on the central Chilean coast, exposed to the northerly gales but otherwise the natural seaward outlet of Santiago, which lies inland behind the coastal range. Under Spanish rule it was a slight place — a roadstead with a few warehouses serving the colony of Chile, at the far end of the Pacific trade and subordinate to Callao and the isthmus route north. The Horn traffic largely passed it by while the old convoy system endured.

Independence in 1818 transformed it. As the first sheltered anchorage a ship reached after rounding Cape Horn into the Pacific, and as the port of a republic that threw open its trade, Valparaiso drew British, North American, and other merchant houses through the 1820s. By the 1830s and 1840s it had become the commercial and financial entrepot of the whole South American Pacific coast, a warehousing and banking centre where cargoes were broken up and redistributed along the coast and across to the wider ocean — the rising port of the region as this chart draws to its close.
sources: Benjamin Vicuna Mackenna, Historia de Valparaiso (1869)
Eduardo Cavieres, Comercio chileno y comerciantes ingleses, 1820-1880 (1988)

sydney.blurb: Founded in 1788 as a penal colony on Gadigal land at Sydney Cove, and a wool-exporting port from the 1820s.
doc: Sydney stands on Port Jackson, one of the world's great natural harbours: a deep, branching drowned valley on the south-eastern coast of Australia, its coves sheltered and its water deep to the shore. The first British settlement was made in 1788 at Sydney Cove, which the Gadigal people whose country this was knew as Warrane; the land belonged to the Gadigal and the neighbouring clans of the Eora, and the colony was planted on it without treaty or purchase. The dispossession of those inhabitants — dulled in colonial language by the fiction of an empty land — was the founding fact of the port, and the smallpox epidemic of 1789 that followed the newcomers killed a great part of the Eora within a year.

The colony was established to receive convicts — men and women transported under sentence from Britain and Ireland, prisoners serving punishment, not goods in a hold — and for its first decades that penal traffic and the government store were its economy. From the 1820s a genuine export trade grew up: fine wool from the flocks of the interior, together with whale and seal oil from the southern fisheries, gave the settlement a return cargo and drew merchant shipping in its own right. By the 1830s and 1840s Sydney had become the principal commercial port of the Australian colonies, still receiving convicts to mid-century but increasingly a trading town on the strength of its wool.
sources: Grace Karskens, The Colony: A History of Early Sydney (2009)
Inga Clendinnen, Dancing with Strangers: Europeans and Australians at First Contact (2003)

pacific-grounds.blurb: The offshore sperm-whale grounds off Peru and Chile, opened in 1788 and worked by British and then overwhelmingly American whalers.
doc: This node marks not a port but a region of open sea: the sperm-whale grounds off the Pacific coast of South America, off Peru and Chile and stretching out into the ocean to the west. They were opened to the European fishery in 1788, when the British whaler Emilia became the first to round Cape Horn and take sperm whales in the South Pacific, and further "offshore grounds" further out to sea were worked in the decades that followed. The whalers themselves carried no home here; they cruised for months and provisioned at the coastal ports and islands — Callao, Paita, Talcahuano, and above all the Galapagos — before returning to the hunt.

British ships led the early fishery, but after 1815 it became overwhelmingly American, prosecuted by the sperm-whaling fleets of Nantucket and New Bedford whose voyages ran three and four years around the world. The grounds fed the demand for spermaceti and whale oil that lit and lubricated the industrialising Atlantic world, and their working built toward the mid-nineteenth-century peak of the American whale fishery. The node is entered here in the manner of the Davis Strait and Smeerenburg grounds — a place ships went to work rather than a harbour anyone held.
sources: Briton Cooper Busch, Whaling Will Never Do for Me: The American Whaleman in the Nineteenth Century (1994)
Edouard A. Stackpole, The Sea-Hunters: The New England Whalemen during Two Centuries, 1635-1835 (1953)

venice.blurb: The Serenissima's lagoon capital, its Arsenal-built galleys and round ships carrying Levant spice, glass, and salt long after Atlantic routes had begun to eclipse the old Mediterranean carrying trade.
doc: Venice sat on the mud islands of a shallow lagoon, its deep-water shipping working from the Bacino di San Marco and the state Arsenal, the largest industrial complex in Europe, where galleys were built and armed on a near-assembly-line system. There was no river and no open roadstead: vessels threaded the lagoon channels past the Lido, and the whole city was its own fortified basin. Through it moved the eastern goods of the Republic's maritime empire, the Stato da Mar, together with the glass of Murano, Istrian and Dalmatian salt, and the woollens that Venice re-exported into the Ottoman lands.

Across 1550 to 1850 the port's fortunes ran steadily downward from a high that was already past. The spice trade had been diverted round the Cape, Ottoman wars stripped away Cyprus (1571) and eventually Crete (1669) and the Morea, and northern carriers took over even the Mediterranean freights. Venice remained a working port and naval power into the eighteenth century, but the Republic fell to Bonaparte in 1797, passing to Austria and then, briefly, to the Napoleonic Kingdom of Italy. The city itself was called Venezia, La Serenissima, or simply the Dominante.
sources: Frederic C. Lane, Venice: A Maritime Republic (1973)
Fernand Braudel, The Mediterranean and the Mediterranean World in the Age of Philip II (1949; trans. 1972)

genoa.blurb: The Ligurian republic of merchant-bankers whose capital, more than its own thinning merchant marine, financed Habsburg Spain and moved American silver through the Mediterranean.
doc: Genoa lay against a steep coast with an artificial harbour sheltered by moles, its old and new piers curving out to enclose a crowded basin below the amphitheatre of the city. It had little hinterland and depended wholly on the sea. In the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries its distinctive business was less the carriage of goods than of money: the Genoese asientos financed the Spanish crown, and the Besançon exchange fairs made the city a clearing house for the silver of the Indies. Alongside the finance ran a real trade in silk, paper, olive oil, coral, and Corsican and Levantine goods.

The republic's commercial marine declined through the period even as its banking endured, and Genoa increasingly saw its freights carried in Dutch, English, and later Ragusan and Tuscan bottoms. War with Savoy, the French bombardment of 1684, and shifting silver flows all pressed on it; the republic ended with the Napoleonic Ligurian Republic (1797) and annexation to France, before passing to Piedmont-Sardinia in 1815. The city was La Superba, Genova, its people the Genoese whose name traders across Europe knew from their bills of exchange.
sources: Thomas A. Kirk, Genoa and the Sea: Policy and Power in an Early Modern Maritime Republic, 1559-1684 (2005)
Fernand Braudel, Civilization and Capitalism, 15th-18th Century (1979; trans. 1981-84)

livorno.blurb: The Grand Duke of Tuscany's free port, its neutral roadstead open to every flag, which was the whole design: a warehouse for the Mediterranean where Jews, Greeks, Armenians, and northern factors traded goods that mostly arrived and left under other colours.
doc: Livorno (Leghorn to the English) was a made port, laid out on marshy coast by the Medici from the 1570s with a new mole, a fortified pentagon, and a planned town behind it. It had no natural depth of hinterland and no great river; its cargoes were drawn instead by the Livornina charters, which granted freedom of religion and of trade to newcomers and made the town a duty-light entrepot. Grain in dearth years, coral, silk, wax, sugar, and above all a vast re-export traffic passed across its quays, and its resident Sephardi, Greek, and Armenian communities, alongside Dutch and English factors, handled much of it.

The town's business model was neutrality and openness, and it prospered as long as its harbour stayed a safe common ground, filling with foreign shipping through the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. Its own Tuscan flag covered only a small marine; most Livorno freights flew Dutch, English, French, or Ragusan colours, exactly as intended. The French occupation and the Continental blockade of the Napoleonic years hit it hard, but the free port revived after 1815 under the restored Grand Duchy, which endured to 1859. The place was known simply as the porto franco, Livorno, or Leghorn.
sources: Corey Tazzara, The Free Port of Livorno and the Transformation of the Mediterranean World, 1574-1790 (2017)
Fernand Braudel, The Mediterranean and the Mediterranean World in the Age of Philip II (1949; trans. 1972)

marseille.blurb: France's Mediterranean gateway, its old harbour privileged by crown edict as the kingdom's channel for the Levant trade, importing Ottoman cotton, silk, hides, and drugs and shipping out the woollens of Languedoc.
doc: Marseille worked from the Vieux-Port, a long, narrow, well-sheltered inlet flanked by forts, effectively a fortified basin without a river to silt it. From the mid-seventeenth century royal policy made it the mandatory transit point for French commerce with the Ottoman Levant: goods for the echelles had by edict to pass through Marseille's lazaret and customs, and its Chamber of Commerce and consuls organised the trade. Inbound came Levantine cotton and cotton yarn, raw silk, mohair, hides, wax, coffee from Egypt, and drugs; outbound went the light woollen cloths (draps) of Languedoc, along with sugar, coffee, and colonial goods once the Antilles trade grew.

The port rose strongly across the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries as the dominant Christian carrier in the Levant, its quarantine station guarding against the plague that nonetheless struck catastrophically in 1720. The Revolution and the wars with Britain badly disrupted its shipping, but Marseille recovered in the nineteenth century as France's chief Mediterranean and, increasingly, colonial port. It was always simply Marseille, the cite phoceenne to the learned, its merchants the negociants who ran the Levant houses.
sources: Junko Therese Takeda, Between Crown and Commerce: Marseille and the Early Modern Mediterranean (2011)
Charles Carriere, Negociants marseillais au XVIIIe siecle (1973)

naples.blurb: The teeming capital of its kingdom and one of the largest cities in Europe, its bay crowded with the coasting craft that fed it grain, oil, and silk while its own deep-sea marine stayed modest.
doc: Naples lay on a magnificent bay under Vesuvius, with an open roadstead improved by the mole that reached out below the Castel Nuovo and the Castel dell'Ovo. It was above all a consuming city, one of Europe's largest, and its harbour traffic was dominated by the coastwise and short-sea trades that provisioned it: grain from Apulia and Sicily, oil, wine, cheese, and the raw and worked silk of the Mezzogiorno. Spanish, Genoese, Ragusan, and later English and Dutch vessels carried much of the long-distance business; the kingdom's own flag covered a smaller marine.

As a Spanish viceroyalty to 1707, then briefly Austrian, and from 1734 the seat of an independent Bourbon monarchy, Naples remained a great political capital whose port served consumption more than entrepot commerce. Grain shortages, the revolt of 1647, and the plague of 1656 marked its worst years; the eighteenth century brought reform and modest commercial growth under the Bourbons, interrupted by the Napoleonic decade of 1806-15. The city was Napoli, capital of the Regno, its harbour the mole beneath the fortresses.
sources: John A. Marino, Pastoral Economics in the Kingdom of Naples (1988)
Fernand Braudel, The Mediterranean and the Mediterranean World in the Age of Philip II (1949; trans. 1972)

barcelona.blurb: Catalonia's harbour under the Spanish crown, long shut out of the Seville-Cadiz monopoly on the Indies and thrown back on Mediterranean and coasting trades until the eighteenth century reopened Atlantic and colonial commerce to it.
doc: Barcelona's shipping worked from a shallow shore improved over centuries by an artificial mole and quays, the harbour repeatedly dredged and extended against silting. Through the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries the city was excluded from the legal trade of the Carrera de Indias, which was reserved to Andalusian ports, and its commerce was chiefly Mediterranean: wine and brandy, salt, wool and woollen cloth, dried fruit, and the coasting exchange with the Balearics, Valencia, Italy, and the Levant. It also built and manned galleys for the crown.

The city suffered heavily in the Catalan revolt (1640-52) and again in the War of the Spanish Succession, falling to the Bourbons in 1714. But the later eighteenth century transformed it: Catalan trade with America was legalised (fully by the 1778 comercio libre decrees), and Barcelona boomed on colonial re-export, brandy, and the printed-cotton (indianes) industry, becoming the industrial heart of Spain in the nineteenth century. It was always Barcelona, chief city of the Principat de Catalunya under the crown of Spain.
sources: J. K. J. Thomson, A Distinctive Industrialization: Cotton in Barcelona, 1728-1832 (1992)
Fernand Braudel, The Mediterranean and the Mediterranean World in the Age of Philip II (1949; trans. 1972)

istanbul.blurb: The Ottoman capital astride the Bosporus and one of the largest cities on earth, its provisioning drew grain, timber, and goods from the whole Black Sea and eastern Mediterranean; among the human beings trafficked into it were captives sold in its slave market, recorded here soberly, never as trade.
doc: Istanbul commanded the Golden Horn, a deep, sheltered natural harbour, and the Bosporus channel between the Black Sea and the Sea of Marmara, with quays, custom houses (the gumruk), and shipyards at Kasimpasa lining the inlet. As the seat of the sultan and one of the largest cities in the world, it was an immense consumer, and the machinery of the state bent trade toward feeding it: Egyptian and Black Sea grain, timber and naval stores, wool, hides, and slaves were drawn in, much of the carriage in Greek- and Armenian-crewed bottoms under the Ottoman flag. Its own manufactures, spices trans-shipped from the Red Sea, and the goods of the caravan routes moved outward.

The city's history across the period is one of a great capital rather than a competitive trading port: its commerce served empire and provisioning, and it stood outside the European rankings that could not represent so state-directed a metabolism, which is why the chart carries it as its declared-boundary exemplar. Among the flows converging on it was the traffic in enslaved people brought from the Black Sea, the Caucasus, and Africa and sold in the city's markets: this is set down as the coerced movement of human beings, not as a commodity trade. The city was Konstantiniyye in the chancery, Istanbul in speech, and Tsargrad or simply the City to its neighbours.
sources: Halil Inalcik, The Ottoman Empire: The Classical Age, 1300-1600 (1973)
Halil Inalcik and Donald Quataert (eds.), An Economic and Social History of the Ottoman Empire, 1300-1914 (1994)

blurb.text: Capital of the Ottoman Regency and the most feared corsairing base of the western Mediterranean; its licit exports were grain, wool, hides, wax, and coral, while its corsairs seized ships and captives, and the Christians held for ransom or forced labour in the city are recorded here soberly, as people taken against their will, never as cargo.
The seat of French Algeria after the conquest of 1830, its harbour reworked into a colonial and military port as the old Regency and its corsairing were extinguished.

doc: Algiers rose in tiers up a hillside above a harbour that the corsairs had made by joining offshore islets to the shore with a mole (the name al-Jaza'ir, 'the islands', records them), enclosing a basin under the guns of the Penon and the casbah. Its licit seaborne trade, the flow this chart sails, was in grain, wool, hides, wax, dates, and coral shipped to Marseille, Livorno, and the Levant. But the Regency's notoriety, and much of its treasury, came from privateering: its galleys and later sailing corsairs took merchant ships and coastal captives across the Mediterranean and into the Atlantic. The people so seized were held in the bagnios for ransom or set to forced labour, and are set down here as human beings taken by force, not as a trade good or a spectacle.

As an Ottoman Regency governed by pashas, then aghas, and from 1671 by deys, Algiers was effectively autonomous, its power resting on the corsair captains (the reis) and the janissary militia. European states paid tribute and negotiated redemption of captives through religious orders and consuls. The corsairing waned in the eighteenth century and was pressed hard after 1815, and in 1830 a French expedition took the city, ending the Regency; from 1830 Algiers became the capital of French Algeria. Under the Ottomans it was Cezayir or al-Jaza'ir; the Europeans called it Algiers or Alger.
sources: Robert C. Davis, Christian Slaves, Muslim Masters: White Slavery in the Mediterranean, the Barbary Coast, and Italy, 1500-1800 (2003)
Fernand Braudel, The Mediterranean and the Mediterranean World in the Age of Philip II (1949; trans. 1972)

tunis.blurb: The Regency capital behind its lagoon, an Ottoman then Husainid state that exported grain, oil, wool, and the red caps of its own manufacture, and whose corsairs also took ships and captives, the enslaved held in the city recorded here soberly rather than as trade.
doc: Tunis proper lies inland behind the shallow Lake of Tunis; its sea gate was La Goulette (Halq al-Wadi), the fortified channel where the chart's dot sits and where sea-going ships anchored and lightered their cargoes up to the city. Its licit exports were wheat and barley, olive oil, wool, hides, wax, dates, and the woollen skullcaps (chechias) for which Tunis was famous across the Ottoman world. Marseillais, Livornese, and other Christian factors traded there under capitulations. Like its Barbary neighbours the Regency also fitted out corsairs, and the captives they seized were held for ransom or forced labour in the city; those people are recorded here as human beings taken by force, not as merchandise.

Ottoman from the final conquest of 1574, Tunis passed under the effective rule of its own beys, and from 1705 the Husainid dynasty governed it as a hereditary, largely autonomous regency that outlasted the period covered here. Its corsairing declined through the eighteenth century and was suppressed under European pressure after 1815, while the grain and oil trade and the cap manufacture continued. The state was the Iyala or Regency of Tunis; the port was La Goulette, the city Tunis.
sources: Robert C. Davis, Christian Slaves, Muslim Masters: White Slavery in the Mediterranean, the Barbary Coast, and Italy, 1500-1800 (2003)
Fernand Braudel, The Mediterranean and the Mediterranean World in the Age of Philip II (1949; trans. 1972)

tripoli.blurb: The westernmost Barbary regency and the Mediterranean sea gate of the Saharan caravan routes, exporting the goods those caravans brought north; like its neighbours it fitted out corsairs, and the captives they seized are recorded here soberly, as people held by force.
doc: Tripoli of Barbary (Tarabulus al-Gharb) stood on a low, exposed shore, its harbour sheltered by reefs and an old Spanish-built castle and mole, an open roadstead more than a deep basin. Its distinctive role was as the maritime terminus of the trans-Saharan caravan trade from Fezzan and the Sudan: gold, ivory, ostrich feathers, senna, and enslaved Africans were carried across the desert to Tripoli, and from the coast went the wool, hides, dates, and barilla of the region. The overland caravan itself is registered in this chart as a silence rather than sailed. The Regency also armed corsairs, and the Christian captives they took were held for ransom or forced labour; they are set down here as human beings taken against their will, never as cargo.

Ottoman from 1551, Tripoli was governed for the eighteenth century (1711-1835) by the semi-independent Karamanli dynasty, whose corsairing drew it into conflict with European powers and the young United States in the wars of 1801-05 and 1815. Ottoman direct rule was restored in 1835. Its caravan trade persisted longest of all the Barbary specialities. The state was the Regency of Tripoli; the town Tarabulus, Tripoli to the Europeans.
sources: Robert C. Davis, Christian Slaves, Muslim Masters: White Slavery in the Mediterranean, the Barbary Coast, and Italy, 1500-1800 (2003)
Ralph A. Austen, Trans-Saharan Africa in World History (2010)

alexandria.blurb: Ottoman Egypt's Mediterranean port and a principal echelle of the Levant trade, funnelling Egyptian grain, rice, flax, and the coffee and spices trans-shipped up from the Red Sea into European bottoms.
doc: Alexandria occupied the isthmus between two harbours, the western (Eunostos) open to Christian shipping and the eastern reserved to Ottoman and Muslim vessels, both remnants of the ancient Ptolemaic port and by this period much decayed and silted. The city itself had shrunk to a fraction of its classical size, linked to the Nile by a canal that determined whether goods could reach it. Through it passed Egyptian wheat and rice, flax and linen, natron, sal ammoniac, and the coffee of Mocha and the spices of the Red Sea trans-shipped down from Cairo, exchanged for the woollens, metals, and silver the European consuls' nations brought. French, Venetian, and English factors kept houses under the capitulations.

For most of the period Alexandria was a modest secondary port of Ottoman Egypt, overshadowed by Cairo and dependent on the fluctuating Nile canal; the French expedition of 1798-1801 and, decisively, the reign of Muhammad Ali (from 1805), who cut the new Mahmudiyya canal in 1820 and made the port his naval and commercial base, began its nineteenth-century revival. Nominally Ottoman throughout, Egypt under Muhammad Ali grew all but independent. The city was al-Iskandariyya, Alexandria to the Europeans, one of the echelles du Levant.
sources: Michael J. Reimer, Colonial Bridgehead: Government and Society in Alexandria, 1807-1882 (1997)
Bruce Masters, The Origins of Western Economic Dominance in the Middle East (1988)

scanderoon.blurb: Iskenderun, the malarial but deep roadstead that served as the sea gate of Aleppo and the chief unloading place of the English Levant Company, where the caravan goods of inland Syria met European shipping.
doc: Scanderoon (Iskenderun, the Europeans' Alexandretta) was less a town than an anchorage: a deep bay at the head of the Gulf of Alexandretta, backed by fever-ridden marshes, chosen because it was the nearest safe roadstead to Aleppo, some three days' caravan inland over the Belen pass. It had no proper harbour works, and ships lay in the open bay while their cargoes were carried up to Aleppo by muleteer caravan. Through it moved the Persian and Syrian raw silk, cotton, galls, and spices that Aleppo gathered, exchanged for the broadcloth, tin, and silver of the English and the woollens of the French and Venetians.

The port lived and died by the fortunes of Aleppo's caravan trade. It was the principal factory station of the English Levant Company in the seventeenth century, its unhealthiness notorious in the consuls' letters, and it declined in the eighteenth as the Persian silk trade faltered, wars disrupted the caravans, and Smyrna drew the Levant business westward. Nominally Ottoman throughout, it was Iskenderun in Turkish, Scanderoon or Alexandretta to the English and Italians, the scala of Aleppo.
sources: Bruce Masters, The Origins of Western Economic Dominance in the Middle East: Mercantilism and the Islamic Economy in Aleppo, 1600-1750 (1988)
Alfred C. Wood, A History of the Levant Company (1935)

smyrna.blurb: Izmir, the great cosmopolitan echelle that overtook all its rivals to become the busiest of the Levant ports, its bay drawing the caravans of Anatolia and Persia down to the quays of its French, English, Dutch, and Venetian factors.
doc: Smyrna (Izmir) commanded a long, deep, well-sheltered gulf on the Aegean coast of Anatolia, with a working waterfront of quays, khans, and consular houses (the 'Frank street') strung along the shore below the old castle. From a modest town in 1550 it grew across the seventeenth century into the pre-eminent echelle of the Levant, as the caravan routes of western Anatolia and, through them, of Persia were pulled toward its bay. Raw cotton and cotton yarn, silk, mohair yarn from Angora, wax, dried fruit (raisins and figs), galls, and opium went out; woollen cloth, tin, colonial goods, and silver came in. Its trade was famously polyglot, handled by Greek, Armenian, Jewish, and 'Frankish' merchant communities under the capitulations.

By the eighteenth century Smyrna was the busiest port of the Ottoman Levant, outstripping Aleppo's Scanderoon as the silk trade shifted and the Aegean fruit and cotton trades grew. Earthquakes, fires, and plague periodically devastated it, but it always rebuilt and remained the region's chief commercial outlet into the nineteenth century, its Christian and Jewish quarters giving it the byname gavur Izmir, 'infidel Smyrna'. Ottoman throughout the period, it was Izmir in Turkish, Smyrna to the Europeans.
sources: Daniel Goffman, Izmir and the Levantine World, 1550-1650 (1990)
Elena Frangakis-Syrett, The Commerce of Smyrna in the Eighteenth Century, 1700-1820 (1992)

blurb.text: A British colonial port on Massachusetts Bay, provisioning the West Indies with fish, timber, and livestock and building ships on its own account.
A leading United States port, its merchants sending vessels to China, the Baltic, and the Mediterranean.

doc: Boston sits on a hilly peninsula reaching into a deep, island-sheltered harbour at the mouth of the Charles and Mystic rivers, an anchorage that made it the principal shipping town of colonial New England from its founding in 1630. It lacked a great cash staple of its own; instead its merchants grew wealthy as intermediaries, carrying dried cod, timber, barrel-staves, horses, and provisions to the sugar islands of the West Indies and returning with molasses, which local distilleries turned into rum. Shipbuilding flourished along the waterfront, and by the early eighteenth century Boston-built and Boston-owned tonnage plied routes to Britain, southern Europe, the wine islands, and the Caribbean.

New England capital was entangled in the Atlantic slave economy: Boston houses fitted out slaving voyages, distilled the rum that was exchanged for captive people on the African coast, and profited from provisioning the plantation islands worked by enslaved labour; some Africans were also held in bondage within the town itself. Boston's commercial primacy slipped behind New York and Philadelphia in the later eighteenth century, and the port suffered acutely under the Revolutionary blockade and the punitive Port Act of 1774. After independence its merchants recovered by pioneering long-distance routes — to Canton, the Pacific Northwest fur coast, the Baltic, and the Mediterranean — that carried the town's shipping through the first half of the nineteenth century.
sources: Bernard Bailyn, The New England Merchants in the Seventeenth Century (1955)
W. Jeffrey Bolster, The Mortal Sea: Fishing the Atlantic in the Age of Sail (2012)

blurb.text: New Amsterdam, the Dutch West India Company's fur and provisioning post at the mouth of the Hudson.
British New York, exporting flour, provisions, and furs and carrying a busy West India trade.
The rising commercial capital of the United States, its harbour the busiest in the country by the 1820s.

doc: New York commands one of the world's finest natural harbours — a broad, deep, ice-rarely bay where the Hudson meets the sea, sheltered by Long Island and reached through the Narrows. The Dutch West India Company founded New Amsterdam on the tip of Manhattan in 1625 as a fur-trading and provisioning post; the town passed to English hands in 1664 and was renamed for the Duke of York. Under British rule it exported wheat flour, bread, and provisions to the West Indies and southern Europe, dealt in furs drawn down the Hudson, and grew steadily as a distribution centre for the middle colonies.

New York's colonial economy was bound up with slavery: the town held one of the largest urban enslaved populations in British North America, and its merchants trafficked in enslaved people and provisioned the plantation colonies. After the Revolution the port's advantages — a superb harbour, the Hudson corridor into the interior, and after 1825 the Erie Canal — carried it past its rivals to become the leading commercial city of the United States. By the 1820s its wharves handled the greatest share of the nation's foreign trade, the packet lines to Liverpool and Le Havre sailed on fixed schedules from its piers, and cotton from the southern ports was funnelled through New York factors on its way to Europe.
sources: Cathy Matson, Merchants and Empire: Trading in Colonial New York (1998)
Robert G. Albion, The Rise of New York Port, 1815–1860 (1939)

blurb.text: William Penn's Quaker city on the Delaware, the great grain and flour port of British North America.
A principal United States port, exporting flour and provisions and pioneering the early China trade.

doc: Philadelphia lies well up the Delaware River, on the neck between the Delaware and the Schuylkill, a freshwater port reached by a long buoyed passage from the capes of the bay. Founded by William Penn in 1682, it drew on the exceptionally productive farmland of Pennsylvania and its neighbouring colonies and became the pre-eminent grain port of British America, shipping wheat, flour, bread, and provisions to the West Indies, southern Europe, and the wine islands. By the eve of the Revolution it was the largest city in the colonies and one of the busiest ports in the British Atlantic, its merchant houses handling a diverse re-export and provisioning trade.

Pennsylvania's commerce, though shaped by a large Quaker mercantile community that turned early against slaveholding, was not separate from the Atlantic slave economy: the provisions Philadelphia shipped fed the enslaved-worked plantations of the Caribbean, and enslaved people were bought and sold in the town before gradual abolition began in 1780. The port's river approach could freeze in hard winters, a standing disadvantage against ice-free rivals. After independence Philadelphia remained a major exporter of flour and a centre of the early United States trade to Canton, though over the first half of the nineteenth century its share of national commerce was overtaken by New York's superior harbour and canal connections.
sources: Thomas M. Doerflinger, A Vigorous Spirit of Enterprise: Merchants and Economic Development in Revolutionary Philadelphia (1986)
Gary B. Nash, The Urban Crucible: Social Change, Political Consciousness, and the Origins of the American Revolution (1979)

blurb.text: The tobacco coast of British Virginia and Maryland, its crop grown by enslaved labour and shipped direct from a thousand private landings.
The tobacco and grain shores of Virginia and Maryland, worked by enslaved people, with Norfolk and Baltimore as their outlets.

doc: The Chesapeake is not a single harbour but a vast drowned river valley, its long tidal estuaries — the James, York, Rappahannock, Potomac, and Patuxent — reaching deep into Virginia and Maryland and allowing ocean-going ships to load almost at the planters' own wharves. English settlement began at Jamestown in 1607, and the bay's economy soon rested on a single export: tobacco, grown for the British and re-export markets and shipped in bulk from innumerable small private landings rather than concentrated at one town. This node stands at the capes for the whole tobacco coast, of which Norfolk and Annapolis were the nearest real ports.

The Chesapeake economy was built on the labour of enslaved people. Through the seventeenth century tobacco was worked increasingly by Africans held in bondage, and by the eighteenth century a large enslaved population — trafficked across the Atlantic and then held and sold within the region — sustained the plantations; the sober fact of that coerced labour underlies every hogshead the bay shipped. As tobacco soils exhausted, planters shifted toward wheat, and after independence Baltimore rose rapidly as a flour and general port at the head of the bay. In the nineteenth century the upper Chesapeake also became a source region for the domestic slave trade, from which enslaved people were carried southwest to the cotton frontier.
sources: Allan Kulikoff, Tobacco and Slaves: The Development of Southern Cultures in the Chesapeake, 1680–1800 (1986)
Lorena S. Walsh, Motives of Honor, Pleasure, and Profit: Plantation Management in the Colonial Chesapeake, 1607–1763 (2010)

blurb.text: St John's and the Newfoundland shore fishery, drawing English, French, and other ships to the cod grounds of the Grand Banks.
Louisbourg, France's fortified fishery base and naval station on Île Royale, guarding the approaches to the St Lawrence.
St John's again, the British Newfoundland cod fishery and its Banks traffic, after Louisbourg's fall and demolition.

doc: This node stands for the cod coast of the northwest Atlantic, where the shallow Grand Banks off Newfoundland held the richest fishery in the early-modern world and drew fishing fleets across the ocean each season. For most of the era the traffic is that of St John's — a small, superbly sheltered harbour entered through a narrow rock gut — and the migratory and resident English fishery that dried salt cod on the Newfoundland shore for the Catholic markets of southern Europe. The chart's single node stands in for that whole coast; the real St John's lies some 500 km to the east of the plotted point.

Between 1713 and 1758 the node instead carries Louisbourg, the fortress town France raised on Île Royale (Cape Breton) after the Treaty of Utrecht ceded Newfoundland and Acadia to Britain. Louisbourg was at once a fishing base, a trans-shipment port linking France, Canada, and the West Indies, and a naval station commanding the Gulf of St Lawrence. It was besieged and taken by a New England expedition in 1745, returned by treaty, taken again by the British in 1758, and then systematically demolished so it could never again shelter a French fleet. Thereafter the cod fishery reverted to its British Newfoundland channels, and the node reads St John's once more.
sources: A. J. B. Johnston, Endgame 1758: The Promise, the Glory, and the Despair of Louisbourg's Last Decade (2007)
Peter E. Pope, Fish into Wine: The Newfoundland Plantation in the Seventeenth Century (2004)

blurb.text: The eastern terminus of the Manila galleon, where Asian silks and porcelain were exchanged for New World silver on the Pacific coast of New Spain.
A quiet harbour of independent Mexico after the galleon trade ended, its great annual fair gone.

doc: Acapulco possesses one of the finest deep-water bays on the Pacific coast of the Americas — an almost landlocked circular harbour ringed by hills, safe in any weather. From 1571 it was the American terminus of the Manila galleon, the single annual (sometimes twice-yearly) trans-Pacific voyage that linked Spanish Manila to New Spain. Each arrival triggered a great trade fair at which the galleon's cargo of Chinese silks, porcelain, spices, and Asian manufactures was exchanged for the silver of the Mexican and Peruvian mines. For most of the year the town was small and sleepy; the silver that crossed the Pacific was carried overland from Acapulco to Veracruz for onward shipment to Spain, which is the point of the connection.

The galleon line ran under the Spanish crown for nearly two and a half centuries, tying the economy of a small Mexican port to Canton and the China trade far more than to the Atlantic. The trade was disrupted by the Napoleonic wars and the Mexican War of Independence, and the last galleon sailed in 1815. With independence in 1821 Acapulco passed to Mexico (for which the chart has no distinct flag, so the Spanish colonial power is carried forward as the nearest available), and without the galleon the harbour lapsed into a long quiet before its later revival as a coastal and, much later, resort port.
sources: William Lytle Schurz, The Manila Galleon (1939)
Arturo Giráldez, The Age of Trade: The Manila Galleons and the Dawn of the Global Economy (2015)

blurb.text: La Nouvelle-Orléans, France's struggling entrepot near the mouth of the Mississippi, outlet for the Louisiana colony.
Nueva Orleans under Spanish rule, its Mississippi trade increasingly fed by the American interior upriver.
The great American cotton and sugar port at the mouth of the Mississippi — and the largest slave market in the United States.

doc: New Orleans stands on the low, flood-prone crescent of the lower Mississippi, roughly 150 km above the river's mouth, on ground so waterlogged that it had to be diked and drained. The French founded it in 1718 as the outlet of their Louisiana colony; it passed to Spain in 1763, briefly back to France, and to the United States with the Louisiana Purchase of 1803. Its whole reason for being was the river: everything grown or made in the vast Mississippi–Ohio–Missouri basin could float down to its levee, and by the 1830s and 40s a solid mile of flatboats, keelboats, and steamboats crowded its front, discharging cotton, sugar, tobacco, flour, and western produce for trans-shipment to the Atlantic and Europe.

New Orleans was also the largest slave market in the United States. It was the principal receiving point of the domestic, coastwise slave trade — the 'second Middle Passage' — by which enslaved people were carried from the Chesapeake and the upper South to the cotton and sugar frontier, trafficked against their will and sold in the city's markets and pens; the chart carries that coastwise movement soberly as its own flow, without value or profit framing. The sugar plantations below the city and the cotton lands upriver on which the port's commerce rested were worked by enslaved labour. Behind its levees the city grew into one of the wealthiest and most cosmopolitan in the antebellum United States, and by the 1840s it ranked among the busiest ports in the country by the value of goods crossing its wharves.
sources: Lawrence N. Powell, The Accidental City: Improvising New Orleans (2012)
Walter Johnson, Soul by Soul: Life Inside the Antebellum Slave Market (1999)

london.blurb: The metropolis of English trade and by the late eighteenth century the busiest port in the world, its Thames-side legal quays and later wet docks handling colonial re-exports, coal, and the traffic of a global empire.
doc: London lies far up the tidal Thames, some sixty miles from the open sea, on a reach where the river was wide and deep enough for ocean shipping yet sheltered from weather and enemy fleets. For most of the era ships discharged at the crowded 'legal quays' below London Bridge or lay at moorings in the Pool of London, lightered ashore; the congestion grew so severe that from 1799 great enclosed wet docks — the West India, London, and East India Docks — were dug to hold the traffic. London dominated English overseas commerce: it was the seat of the East India Company and the great chartered trades, the entrepot through which colonial sugar, tobacco, tea, coffee, and textiles were imported and re-exported across Europe, and the chief market and financial centre of the kingdom.

London's commerce was thoroughly enmeshed in the Atlantic slave economy — its merchants financed slaving voyages, its refineries and warehouses handled the sugar and tobacco produced by enslaved labour, and its insurance and credit underwrote the whole plantation system — even as much of the direct slave-trade fitting-out shifted to Bristol and then Liverpool. The port also drew in the enormous coastwise coal trade from the northeast of England, whose colliers made the Thames one of the most heavily trafficked waterways anywhere. By the later eighteenth century London was reckoned the busiest port in the world, and through the Napoleonic wars and after it remained the commercial heart of Britain's expanding global reach.
sources: Ralph Davis, The Rise of the English Shipping Industry in the Seventeenth and Eighteenth Centuries (1962)
Sarah Palmer, Politics, Shipping and the Repeal of the Navigation Laws (1990)

liverpool.blurb: The rising Atlantic port of northwest England that, by the mid-eighteenth century, became the dominant British slave-trading port, its ships carrying trafficked Africans to the Americas.
doc: Liverpool grew on the Mersey estuary, a tidal river with a fierce range whose exposed anchorage was tamed by the world's first commercial enclosed wet dock, opened in 1715, and the basins that followed along the waterfront. Well placed for the Atlantic and backed by the manufacturing districts of Lancashire and the West Midlands, it rose from a minor creek in 1550 to one of Britain's greatest ports. Its merchants dealt in salt, coal, and colonial produce, and increasingly in the manufactured goods — Manchester textiles, Birmingham metalwares, guns — that fed the African and American trades.

From the 1740s Liverpool became the leading British slave-trading port, overtaking London and Bristol; by the later eighteenth century the majority of British slaving voyages cleared from the Mersey. Its ships carried manufactured goods to the West African coast, where they were exchanged for captive people who were then trafficked across the Atlantic to the plantation colonies against their will, before the vessels returned with sugar, cotton, and other slave-grown produce. After Britain abolished the slave trade in 1807 Liverpool's merchants turned to the raw-cotton trade that supplied the Lancashire mills, and the port continued to expand through the first half of the nineteenth century as the great import gateway of industrial Britain.
sources: David Richardson, Suzanne Schwarz and Anthony Tibbles (eds.), Liverpool and Transatlantic Slavery (2007)
Ralph Davis, The Rise of the English Shipping Industry in the Seventeenth and Eighteenth Centuries (1962)

newcastle.blurb: The Tyneside coal port that shipped 'sea-coal' to London and the Continent — the single largest generator of ship movements in Europe, yet nearly invisible to rankings built on foreign-going tonnage.
doc: Newcastle stands on the Tyne, a few miles up a river lined with coal staithes and keels, at the heart of the great northeast England coalfield. From the sixteenth century onward the collieries of the Tyne and neighbouring Wear shipped 'sea-coal' south to London — whose fires and, later, industries ran on it — and to the ports of eastern England and the near Continent. The coal was loaded into flat-bottomed keels, carried downriver, and trans-shipped into a numerous fleet of colliers that ran a ceaseless coastwise shuttle to the Thames and beyond.

This coasting coal trade was the largest single generator of ship movements in Europe: hundreds of colliers made repeated short voyages every year, far outnumbering the long-distance sailings that fill the trade statistics, and the collier fleet was also a famous nursery of English seamen (James Cook learned his trade in Whitby colliers on this run). Because the chart's rankings rest on a declared basis of foreign-going tonnage, this immense but largely domestic traffic is almost invisible to them — which is precisely why Newcastle is named here. The coal trade grew steadily across the whole era and became one of the foundations of Britain's industrialisation.
sources: John U. Nef, The Rise of the British Coal Industry (1932)
Ralph Davis, The Rise of the English Shipping Industry in the Seventeenth and Eighteenth Centuries (1962)

bristol.blurb: The great western outport of England, long its second city, deeply engaged in the Atlantic and West India trades and, in the earlier eighteenth century, a leading British slave-trading port.
doc: Bristol lies several miles up the winding, muddy Avon from the Severn estuary, on a river with one of the largest tidal ranges in the world; ships worked up to the city quays on the flood and lay aground in the mud at low water until the Floating Harbour was built in 1809 to keep them afloat. For much of the era Bristol was England's second city and its principal Atlantic port outside London, gathering the trade of the west of England and south Wales and sending out ships to the West Indies, the North American colonies, and the wine islands, returning with sugar, tobacco, rum, and other colonial produce.

Bristol was, in the first half of the eighteenth century, one of Britain's leading slave-trading ports: its merchants fitted out voyages that carried manufactured goods to the African coast, trafficked captive people across the Atlantic to the plantation colonies against their will, and brought home the sugar and tobacco produced by enslaved labour. From about the 1740s Liverpool overtook it in the slave trade, and Bristol's relative commercial standing slipped as newer ports rose; but it remained a substantial regional port through the era, its West India and general Atlantic trades continuing after the slave trade was abolished in 1807.
sources: Kenneth Morgan, Bristol and the Atlantic Trade in the Eighteenth Century (1993)
David Richardson, The Bristol Slave Traders: A Collective Portrait (1985)

whydah.blurb: One of the greatest embarkation points of the trans-Atlantic slave trade, where over the era on the order of a million enslaved Africans were forced through the surf onto slave ships on the Bight of Benin's Slave Coast.
doc: Ouidah lay a few miles behind a dangerous, surf-beaten open beach on the Bight of Benin, with no true harbour: ships stood off in the roads while canoes ferried people and goods through the breakers across a treacherous bar. The European powers each kept a fort or factory at the town on sufferance of the African kingdom that ruled the coast — Portugal's Forte São João Baptista, France's Fort Saint-Louis de Grégoy, and England's William's Fort. Sovereignty over the coast belonged to the kingdom of Hueda (Whydah) until the Dahomean conquest of 1727, and to the kingdom of Dahomey thereafter; the forts traded only by leave of these rulers.

For much of the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries Ouidah was among the largest single points of departure of the Middle Passage. Enslaved men, women, and children — on the order of a million over the era, though the figures are estimates and much contested — were marched to the coast, held in barracoons, and carried through the surf to slave ships bound for Brazil, the Caribbean, and the mainland Americas; many died before ever reaching the beach, and many more in the crossing, chained below decks. The trade was run by African rulers and merchants selling to European and, above all after 1700, Brazilian captains from Bahia, who dominated it with rolls of tobacco. Whatever wealth it brought to Dahomey, to the forts' home nations, and to the Bahian traders rested entirely on that coerced and deadly movement of human beings.
sources: Robin Law, Ouidah: The Social History of a West African Slaving Port, 1727–1892 (2004)
Robin Law, The Slave Coast of West Africa, 1550–1750 (1991)

blurb.text: The Portuguese seat in Angola, founded in 1576, and for much of the era the single largest embarkation point of the entire trans-Atlantic slave trade.
Seized by the Dutch West India Company, which held Luanda to ship enslaved Angolans to the sugar plantations it had won in Brazil.
Recovered for Portugal from Brazil in 1648, and once more the chief Angolan port from which enslaved people were carried across the South Atlantic, overwhelmingly to Brazil.

doc: Luanda has one of the few genuinely good natural harbours on the Angolan coast — a sheltered deep bay behind the long low sandy island of Ilha do Cabo. The Portuguese founded the city there in 1576 as São Paulo da Assunção de Loanda, the capital of the colony of Angola; its low-lying, fever-ridden site was notoriously deadly to Europeans. The Dutch West India Company took and held the port from 1641 to 1648, until an expedition sent from Brazil under Salvador de Sá restored it to Portugal — a telling measure of how tightly Angola was bound to the Brazilian economy.

Across the whole era Luanda was the largest single point of departure of the trans-Atlantic slave trade — more enslaved Africans embarked here than at any other port. On the order of two to three million people (the estimates vary and are contested) were carried from Luanda into slavery, the great majority to Brazil, with which Angola formed a direct South-Atlantic circuit that the Brazilian planter economy depended upon. Captives were marched to the coast from deep in the interior, held in the city, and shipped in appalling and often lethal conditions across the ocean. The Portuguese and Luso-African merchant houses and the colony itself lived off this traffic in human beings, which continued, increasingly in defiance of treaty and law, into the 1840s and beyond.
sources: Joseph C. Miller, Way of Death: Merchant Capitalism and the Angolan Slave Trade, 1730–1830 (1988)
Roquinaldo Ferreira, Cross-Cultural Exchange in the Atlantic World: Angola and Brazil during the Era of the Slave Trade (2012)

blurb.text: São Jorge da Mina, Portugal's great Gold Coast castle, raised in 1482 for the West African gold trade and used also as a holding place for enslaved Africans awaiting shipment.
Stormed by the Dutch West India Company in 1637 and made the capital of the Dutch Gold Coast, dealing in gold and, increasingly, in enslaved people carried across the Atlantic.

doc: Elmina — from the Portuguese 'a Mina', the mine — stands on a rocky promontory at the mouth of the Benya lagoon on the Gold Coast, in modern Ghana. São Jorge da Mina castle, built in 1482, was the oldest and largest European fortification in sub-Saharan Africa; the coast has no sheltered harbour, so ships anchored in the open roads while canoes worked the surf. The Dutch West India Company stormed the castle from the Portuguese in 1637 and held it, with the neighbouring Fort Coenraadsburg on the hill above, as the headquarters of the Dutch Gold Coast until it passed to Britain in 1872.

For its first century and a half Elmina was above all a gold entrepôt — the metal that gave the coast its name — traded by the Portuguese and then the Dutch with the Akan states of the interior. But the castle was throughout also a slaving station: its dungeons held enslaved Africans awaiting the Middle Passage, and as Atlantic demand for enslaved labour rose the human trade came to rival the gold. The prosperity Elmina brought the Portuguese crown and then the Dutch company was built on both the gold and the people carried away from this coast; captives held in its cells were shipped across the ocean in conditions that killed many on the passage.
sources: Harvey M. Feinberg, Africans and Europeans in West Africa: Elminans and Dutchmen on the Gold Coast during the Eighteenth Century (1989)
A. van Dantzig, Forts and Castles of Ghana (1980)

saint-louis.blurb: France's oldest West African settlement, at the mouth of the Senegal — a trading base for gum arabic and gold, and a principal point from which enslaved Senegambians were shipped across the Atlantic.
doc: Saint-Louis occupies a narrow, low sandy island in the Senegal River just above its mouth, sheltered from the Atlantic surf by the river spit but guarded by a dangerous bar at the entrance that only small craft could cross safely. French merchants founded it in 1659 as the seat of the French concession in Senegal, worked in turn by the Compagnie du Sénégal, the Compagnie des Indes, and the crown.
