The British captured and held it during the Seven Years' War, from 1758 to 1779, and again in 1809–1817, before it returned to France on each occasion; France remained the dominant power across the era.

The Senegal gave French traders access to the gum arabic of the river valley — a commodity much in demand for European textile finishing — and to gold and hides. But Saint-Louis was also, through the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, a principal Atlantic embarkation point: enslaved Senegambians, bought from and traded through African middlemen up the river, were held on the island and shipped across the ocean, most to the French Caribbean, many dying in the crossing. The town's distinctive community of signares — African and mixed-race women traders — and its merchant houses lived off both the gum and the trade in human beings. After France abolished the slave trade, gum and later groundnuts sustained the town.
sources: James F. Searing, West African Slavery and Atlantic Commerce: The Senegal River Valley, 1700–1860 (1993)
Boubacar Barry, Senegambia and the Atlantic Slave Trade (1998)

bahia.blurb: Brazil's first capital and its great sugar port — a wealth built on the forced labour of enslaved Africans, and itself one of the largest disembarkation markets of the trans-Atlantic slave trade.
doc: Salvador stands on a steep bluff above the Baía de Todos os Santos (All Saints' Bay), one of the largest and best natural harbours in the Atlantic — a vast sheltered bay with an upper and a lower town linked by steep roads. Founded in 1549 as the capital of Portuguese Brazil, which it remained until the seat moved to Rio in 1763, it commanded the sugar-planting Recôncavo around the bay. The Dutch briefly seized the city in 1624–1625 before a Spanish-Portuguese fleet recovered it.

The wealth of Bahia — sugar in the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries, then tobacco and gold — rested on the forced labour of enslaved Africans on the plantations of the Recôncavo, and Salvador was itself one of the greatest disembarkation markets of the entire trans-Atlantic slave trade. Brazil received more enslaved Africans than anywhere else in the Americas, and Bahia took a large share: over the era on the order of a million and a half or more people (estimates vary and are contested) were landed here, many already dead or dying from the crossing. Bahian tobacco traders in turn dominated the slave trade at Ouidah on the African coast, running a direct South-Atlantic circuit. Brazil became independent in 1822, and the traffic in people continued illegally for decades after.
sources: Stuart B. Schwartz, Sugar Plantations in the Formation of Brazilian Society: Bahia, 1550–1835 (1985)
Pierre Verger, Trade Relations between the Bight of Benin and Bahia from the 17th to the 19th Century (1976)

blurb.text: Portugal's richest sugar captaincy, its wealth produced by the forced labour of enslaved Africans on the coastal engenhos.
Seized by the Dutch West India Company as the heart of Dutch Brazil, ruled from Mauritsstad under Johan Maurits — the sugar and the slaving carried on under the Company's flag.
Recovered by Portugal after the Pernambucan revolt; Recife remained a great sugar and slave-trading port of the Brazilian northeast.

doc: The port of Pernambuco is Recife — the name means 'reef' — sheltered behind the long line of sandstone reef that gives the town its natural breakwater, at the easternmost point of the Americas. Olinda on its hill was the old captaincy capital; Recife the port below grew into the city, above all under Dutch rule as Mauritsstad in 1630–1654, when Johan Maurits van Nassau laid out its canals and bridges. The Dutch West India Company held it as the capital of Dutch Brazil until a Portuguese-Brazilian revolt drove them out in 1654.

Pernambuco was, in the sixteenth and early seventeenth centuries, the richest sugar-producing region of the Atlantic world, and its wealth was produced by the forced labour of enslaved Africans on the engenhos of the coastal plain. Recife was accordingly a major disembarkation point of the trans-Atlantic slave trade throughout the era, landing many hundreds of thousands of enslaved people carried across the ocean, chiefly from Angola and the Bight of Benin, in conditions that killed many on the passage. Sugar, and later cotton, built the fortunes of the captaincy's planters and Recife's merchants on that coerced labour. Brazil became independent in 1822.
sources: C. R. Boxer, The Dutch in Brazil, 1624–1654 (1957)
Stuart B. Schwartz, Sugar Plantations in the Formation of Brazilian Society: Bahia, 1550–1835 (1985)

rio.blurb: The outlet of the Minas Gerais gold fields and, in the eighteenth century, the largest disembarkation port of the entire trans-Atlantic slave trade — the enslaved labour on which both the mining and the port depended.
doc: Rio de Janeiro sits on the vast, mountain-ringed Guanabara Bay — one of the world's great natural harbours, a deep sheltered anchorage entered past the Sugarloaf. Founded in 1565, it grew modestly until the discovery of gold in the interior of Minas Gerais around 1700 turned it into the shipping point for the gold fleets, and it replaced Salvador as the capital of Brazil in 1763. In 1808 the Portuguese court itself, fleeing Napoleon, removed to Rio, which became the seat of the Portuguese monarchy and then, from 1822, the capital of independent Brazil.

Rio's eighteenth-century boom rested on two things bound together: the gold and diamonds of Minas Gerais, and the enslaved Africans whose forced labour dug them and worked the port and the surrounding plantations. Rio became the single largest disembarkation point of the entire trans-Atlantic slave trade — over the era well over a million and a half enslaved people (the estimates vary and are contested) were landed on its shores, above all at the Valongo market, carried across the South Atlantic mainly from Angola, many dying on the passage or soon after landing. The merchant houses of Rio, deeply invested in the Angola trade, grew rich on it; the traffic continued, increasingly in defiance of treaty and law, into the 1830s and 1840s.
sources: Mary C. Karasch, Slave Life in Rio de Janeiro, 1808–1850 (1987)
Manolo Florentino, Em Costas Negras: uma história do tráfico de escravos entre a África e o Rio de Janeiro (1997)

montevideo.blurb: The deep-water gate of the Río de la Plata — a Spanish naval and hide-trading port, and the estuary's licensed landing station for enslaved Africans in the late eighteenth century.
doc: Montevideo has the best natural harbour on the Río de la Plata: a genuine sheltered deep-water bay on the north (Banda Oriental) shore, superior to the shallow open roadstead of Buenos Aires across the estuary. The Spanish founded it in 1724 expressly to hold the region against the Portuguese stronghold of Colónia do Sacramento, and it grew into a fortified naval base — the Apostadero — and the estuary's principal deep-water port.

Montevideo shipped the pastoral produce of the Banda Oriental — salted hides, tallow, and dried salt beef (charque), the last of it feeding enslaved plantation workforces elsewhere — and, as the Plata's authorized port of entry, received the enslaved Africans imported into the region in the late eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries: a smaller traffic than Brazil's, but carried in the same coerced and deadly conditions. Britain briefly held the town in 1807; Spanish rule ended in the revolutionary wars from 1814; the region was annexed as the Cisplatine province of Brazil in 1817–1828, and Montevideo became the capital of independent Uruguay in 1828.
sources: Alex Borucki, From Shipmates to Soldiers: Emancipation and Slavery in the Río de la Plata (2015)
John Lynch, The Spanish American Revolutions, 1808–1826 (1973)

cadiz.blurb: The monopoly gateway of Spain's American trade after 1717 — the Carrera de Indias hub through which the silver of the Indies flowed into Europe.
doc: Cádiz occupies the tip of a long, narrow spit enclosing a large sheltered bay on Spain's Atlantic coast near the mouth of the Guadalquivir — a superb deep-water harbour, all but surrounded by sea and easily fortified. It supplanted upriver Seville as the seat of the Casa de Contratación and the head of the Carrera de Indias in 1717, once the silting Guadalquivir could no longer take the growing ships of the American trade.

For the eighteenth century Cádiz was the sole legal gateway of Spain's monopoly trade with its American empire — the point through which the silver of Mexico and Peru entered Europe and European manufactures were shipped to the Indies. It grew into one of the wealthiest and most cosmopolitan cities in Spain, home to foreign merchant colonies — Genoese, Irish, French — who handled much of the commerce. The comercio libre reforms after 1778 opened the American trade to other Spanish ports, and the loss of the mainland American colonies in the 1810s and 1820s broke the city's monopoly prosperity.
sources: Antonio García-Baquero González, Cádiz y el Atlántico, 1717–1778 (1976)
Stanley J. Stein and Barbara H. Stein, Silver, Trade, and War: Spain and America in the Making of Early Modern Europe (2000)

lisbon.blurb: Portugal's capital and great ocean port — the European terminus of the carreira da Índia and of the Brazil fleets that brought Brazilian gold and sugar home.
doc: Lisbon lies on the north bank of the broad Tagus estuary, a few miles above its Atlantic mouth — one of Europe's finest natural harbours, a deep sheltered basin (the Mar da Palha) capable of holding whole fleets, guarded by the Torre de Belém. From here the carreira da Índia sailed for Goa and the Brazil fleets for Salvador and Rio. The great earthquake and tsunami of 1 November 1755 destroyed much of the lower city and killed tens of thousands; it was rebuilt on Pombal's grid plan, the Baixa Pombalina.

Lisbon was the metropolitan hub of the Portuguese seaborne empire — the entrepôt through which Asian spices came in the sixteenth century and, above all, the wealth of Brazil in the seventeenth and eighteenth: Brazilian sugar and tobacco, and then the gold and diamonds of Minas Gerais, much of it flowing on to Britain under the Methuen treaty in exchange for manufactures. That Brazilian wealth rested on the enslaved African labour of the plantations and mines. In 1807–1808 the court fled the French invasion for Rio, an extraordinary reversal that made a colony the seat of empire; Lisbon recovered as capital after 1821.
sources: A. R. Disney, A History of Portugal and the Portuguese Empire, vol. 2 (2009)
C. R. Boxer, The Portuguese Seaborne Empire, 1415–1825 (1969)

porto.blurb: Portugal's second city and the port of the Douro — the shipping point of port wine, above all to England.
doc: Porto (Oporto) stands on the steep north bank of the Douro a little above its mouth; the river bar was notoriously dangerous, and the wine lodges and quays clustered across the water at Vila Nova de Gaia. It is Portugal's second city and the commercial capital of the north.

Porto's fortunes were built on the wine of the Douro valley upstream — fortified 'port' wine, whose trade was dominated by an English and Scottish merchant community established in the city and shipping it above all to Britain under the preferential tariffs of the 1703 Methuen treaty; the British Factory House in Porto was the institutional heart of the trade. The town also handled the region's textiles and provisions and northern Portugal's heavy emigrant traffic to Brazil. It was a centre of liberal resistance, famously enduring the Siege of Porto in 1832–1833 during the Portuguese civil war.
sources: A. D. Francis, The Wine Trade (1972)
Sarah Bradford, The Englishman's Wine: The Story of Port (1969)
